**Uganda**,[[b]](#cite_note-14) officially the **Republic of Uganda**,[[c]](#cite_note-15) is a [landlocked country](/wiki/Landlocked_country "Landlocked country") in [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa"). It is bordered to the east by [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), to the north by [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan"), to the west by the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo"), to the south-west by [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda"), and to the south by [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania"). The southern part includes a substantial portion of [Lake Victoria](/wiki/Lake_Victoria "Lake Victoria"), shared with Kenya and Tanzania. Uganda is in the [African Great Lakes](/wiki/African_Great_Lakes "African Great Lakes") region, lies within the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile") basin, and has a varied [equatorial climate](/wiki/Equatorial_climate "Equatorial climate"). As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Uganda&action=edit), it had a population of 45.9 million, of whom 1.8 million live in the capital and largest city, [Kampala](/wiki/Kampala "Kampala").[[9]](#cite_note-census24-10)

Uganda is named after the [Buganda kingdom](/wiki/Buganda "Buganda"), which encompasses a large portion of the south, including Kampala, and whose language [Luganda](/wiki/Luganda "Luganda") is widely spoken; the official language is English. The region was populated by people of various ethnicities before Bantu and Nilotic groups arrived around 3,000 years ago. These new inhabitants established influential kingdoms such as the [Empire of Kitara](/wiki/Empire_of_Kitara "Empire of Kitara"). The arrival of [Arab](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") traders in the 1830s and British explorers in the late 19th century marked the beginning of foreign influence. The British established the [Protectorate of Uganda](/wiki/Protectorate_of_Uganda "Protectorate of Uganda") in 1894, setting the stage for future political dynamics. Uganda gained independence in 1962, with [Milton Obote](/wiki/Milton_Obote "Milton Obote") as the first prime minister. The 1966 [Mengo Crisis](/wiki/Mengo_Crisis "Mengo Crisis") marked a significant conflict with the Buganda kingdom, as well as the country's conversion from a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") to a [presidential system](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system"). [Idi Amin](/wiki/Idi_Amin "Idi Amin")'s military coup in 1971 led to a brutal regime characterised by mass killings and economic decline, until his overthrow in 1979.

[Yoweri Museveni](/wiki/Yoweri_Museveni "Yoweri Museveni")'s [National Resistance Movement](/wiki/National_Resistance_Movement "National Resistance Movement") (NRM) took power in 1986 after a [six-year guerrilla war](/wiki/Ugandan_Bush_War "Ugandan Bush War"). While Museveni's rule resulted in stability and economic growth, political oppression and human rights abuses continued. The abolition of presidential term limits as well as allegations of electoral fraud and repression have raised concerns about Uganda's democratic future. Museveni was elected president in the [2011](/wiki/2011_Ugandan_general_election "2011 Ugandan general election"), [2016](/wiki/2016_Ugandan_general_election "2016 Ugandan general election"), and [2021](/wiki/2021_Ugandan_general_election "2021 Ugandan general election") general elections. Human rights issues, corruption, and regional conflicts, such as involvement in the Congo Wars and the struggle against the [Lord's Resistance Army](/wiki/Lord%27s_Resistance_Army "Lord's Resistance Army") (LRA), continue to challenge Uganda. Despite this, it has made progress in education and health, improving literacy and reducing HIV infection, though challenges in maternal health and gender inequality persist.

Geographically, Uganda is diverse, with volcanic hills, mountains, and lakes, including [Lake Victoria](/wiki/Lake_Victoria "Lake Victoria"), the world's second-largest freshwater lake. The country has significant natural resources, including fertile agricultural land and untapped [oil reserves](/wiki/Oil_reserve "Oil reserve"), contributing to its economic development. The service sector dominates the economy, surpassing agriculture. Uganda's rich [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity"), with national parks and wildlife reserves, attracts tourism, a vital sector for the economy. Uganda is a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), the [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union"), [G77](/wiki/Group_of_77 "Group of 77"), the [East African Community](/wiki/East_African_Community "East African Community"), and the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation").

## History

Main article: [History of Uganda](/wiki/History_of_Uganda "History of Uganda")

### Precolonial Uganda

Main article: [Early history of Uganda](/wiki/Early_history_of_Uganda "Early history of Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Drawing_showing_a_caesarean_operation_taking_place_in_Uganda_Wellcome_M0001058.jpg/250px-Drawing_showing_a_caesarean_operation_taking_place_in_Uganda_Wellcome_M0001058.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drawing_showing_a_caesarean_operation_taking_place_in_Uganda_Wellcome_M0001058.jpg)

A [caesarean section](/wiki/Caesarean_section "Caesarean section") performed by indigenous healers in Kahura, in the kingdom of [Bunyoro](/wiki/Bunyoro "Bunyoro")[[13]](#cite_note-How_this_African_kingdom_performed_its_cesarean_section_in_1879-16) (present-day Uganda) as observed by medical missionary [Robert William Felkin](/wiki/Robert_William_Felkin "Robert William Felkin") in 1879[[14]](#cite_note-17)

Much of Uganda was inhabited by [Central Sudanic](/wiki/Central_Sudanic_languages "Central Sudanic languages")- and [Kuliak](/wiki/Kuliak_languages "Kuliak languages")-speaking farmers and herders until 3,000 years ago, when [Bantu speakers](/wiki/Bantu_languages "Bantu languages") arrived in the south and [Nilotic speakers](/wiki/Nilotic_languages "Nilotic languages") arrived in the northeast. By 1500 AD, they had all been assimilated into [Bantu-speaking cultures](/wiki/Bantu_peoples "Bantu peoples") south of [Mount Elgon](/wiki/Mount_Elgon "Mount Elgon"), the [Nile River](/wiki/Nile_River "Nile River"), and [Lake Kyoga](/wiki/Lake_Kyoga "Lake Kyoga").[[15]](#cite_note-18) According to [oral tradition](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition") and archaeological studies, the [Empire of Kitara](/wiki/Empire_of_Kitara "Empire of Kitara") covered an important part of the [Great Lakes Area](/wiki/Great_Lakes_of_Africa "Great Lakes of Africa"), from the northern lakes [Albert](/wiki/Lake_Albert_(Uganda) "Lake Albert (Uganda)") and [Kyoga](/wiki/Lake_Kyoga "Lake Kyoga") to the southern lakes [Victoria](/wiki/Lake_Victoria "Lake Victoria") and [Tanganyika](/wiki/Lake_Tanganyika "Lake Tanganyika").[[16]](#cite_note-19) Kitara is claimed as the antecedent of the [Tooro](/wiki/Tooro_Kingdom "Tooro Kingdom"), [Ankole](/wiki/Ankole "Ankole"), and [Busoga](/wiki/Busoga "Busoga") kingdoms.[[17]](#cite_note-Mwambutsya-20) Some [Luo](/wiki/Luo_people#Uganda "Luo people") invaded Kitara and assimilated with the Bantu society there, establishing the Biito dynasty of the current [Omukama](/wiki/Omukama_of_Bunyoro "Omukama of Bunyoro") (ruler) of [Bunyoro-Kitara](/wiki/Bunyoro "Bunyoro").[[18]](#cite_note-babito-21)

[Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") traders moved into the land from the Indian Ocean coast of East Africa in the 1830s for trade and commerce.[[19]](#cite_note-22) In the late 1860s, [Bunyoro](/wiki/Bunyoro "Bunyoro") in Mid-Western Uganda found itself threatened from the north by Egyptian-sponsored agents.[[20]](#cite_note-23) Unlike the Arab traders from the East African coast who sought trade, these agents were promoting foreign conquest. In 1869, [Khedive](/wiki/Khedive "Khedive") [Ismail Pasha](/wiki/Ismail_of_Egypt "Ismail of Egypt") of Egypt, seeking to annex the territories north of the borders of [Lake Victoria](/wiki/Lake_Victoria "Lake Victoria") and east of [Lake Albert](/wiki/Lake_Albert_(Africa) "Lake Albert (Africa)") and "south of [Gondokoro](/wiki/Gondokoro "Gondokoro")",[[21]](#cite_note-Baker_1879-24) sent a British explorer, [Samuel Baker](/wiki/Samuel_Baker "Samuel Baker"), on a military expedition to the frontiers of Northern Uganda, with the objective of suppressing the slave-trade there and opening the way to commerce and "civilization". The Banyoro resisted Baker, who had to fight a desperate battle to secure his retreat. Baker regarded the resistance as an act of treachery, and he denounced the Banyoro in a book (*Ismailia – A Narrative Of The Expedition To Central Africa For The Suppression Of Slave Trade, Organised By Ismail, Khadive Of Egypt* (1874))[[21]](#cite_note-Baker_1879-24) that was widely read in Britain. Later, the British arrived in Uganda with a predisposition against the kingdom of [Bunyoro](/wiki/Bunyoro-Kitara "Bunyoro-Kitara") and sided with the kingdom of [Buganda](/wiki/Buganda "Buganda"). This eventually cost Bunyoro half of its territory, which was given to Buganda as a reward from the British. Two of the numerous "lost counties" were [restored to Bunyoro after independence](/wiki/1964_Ugandan_lost_counties_referendum "1964 Ugandan lost counties referendum").

In the 1860s, while Arabs sought influence from the north, British explorers searching for the source of the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile")[[22]](#cite_note-Stanley-25) arrived in Uganda. They were followed by British Anglican missionaries who arrived in the kingdom of Buganda in 1877 and French Catholic missionaries in 1879. This situation gave rise to the death of the [Uganda Martyrs](/wiki/Uganda_Martyrs "Uganda Martyrs") in 1885—after the conversion of [Muteesa I](/wiki/Muteesa_I_of_Buganda "Muteesa I of Buganda") and much of his court, and the succession of his [anti-Christian](/wiki/Anti-Christian_sentiment "Anti-Christian sentiment") son [Mwanga.](/wiki/Mwanga_II_of_Buganda "Mwanga II of Buganda")[[23]](#cite_note-Background-26) The British government chartered the [Imperial British East Africa Company](/wiki/Imperial_British_East_Africa_Company "Imperial British East Africa Company") (IBEAC) to negotiate trade agreements in the region beginning in 1888.[[24]](#cite_note-Snakes-27)

From 1886, there was a series of religious wars in Buganda, initially between Muslims and Christians and then, from 1890, between "ba-Ingleza" Protestants and "ba-Fransa" Catholics. These factions were named after the imperial powers with which they were aligned.[[25]](#cite_note-28)[[26]](#cite_note-29) Because of civil unrest and financial burdens, IBEAC claimed that it was unable to "maintain their occupation" in the region.[[27]](#cite_note-30) British commercial interests were ardent to protect the trade route of the Nile, which prompted the British government to annex Buganda and adjoining territories to create the Uganda Protectorate in 1894.[[24]](#cite_note-Snakes-27): 3–4[[28]](#cite_note-31)

### Uganda Protectorate (1894–1962)

Main article: [Protectorate of Uganda](/wiki/Protectorate_of_Uganda "Protectorate of Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Flag_of_the_Uganda_Protectorate.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Uganda_Protectorate.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Uganda_Protectorate.svg)

Flag of the [Protectorate of Uganda](/wiki/Protectorate_of_Uganda "Protectorate of Uganda")

The [Protectorate of Uganda](/wiki/Protectorate_of_Uganda "Protectorate of Uganda") was a [protectorate](/wiki/Protectorate "Protectorate") of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") from 1894 to 1962. In 1893, the [Imperial British East Africa Company](/wiki/Imperial_British_East_Africa_Company "Imperial British East Africa Company") transferred its administration rights of territory consisting mainly of the Kingdom of [Buganda](/wiki/Buganda "Buganda") to the British government. The [IBEAC](/wiki/Imperial_British_East_Africa_Company "Imperial British East Africa Company") relinquished its control over Uganda after Ugandan internal religious wars had driven it into bankruptcy.[[29]](#cite_note-32)

In 1894, the Uganda Protectorate was established, and the territory was extended beyond the borders of Buganda by signing more treaties with the other kingdoms ([Toro](/wiki/Toro_(kingdom) "Toro (kingdom)") in 1900,[[30]](#cite_note-33) [Ankole](/wiki/Ankole "Ankole") in 1901, and [Bunyoro](/wiki/Bunyoro "Bunyoro") in 1933[[31]](#cite_note-34)) to an area that roughly corresponds to that of present-day Uganda.[[32]](#cite_note-35)

The status of [protectorate](/wiki/Protectorate "Protectorate") meant that Uganda retained a degree of self-government that would have otherwise been limited under a full colonial administration, compared to neighbouring [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), which had been made a colony.[[33]](#cite_note-36)

In the 1890s, 32,000 labourers from British India were [recruited to East Africa](/wiki/Indian_diaspora_in_East_Africa "Indian diaspora in East Africa") under indentured labour contracts to construct the [Uganda Railway](/wiki/Uganda_Railway "Uganda Railway").[[34]](#cite_note-37) Most of the surviving Indians returned home, but 6,724 decided to remain in East Africa after the line's completion.[[35]](#cite_note-38) Subsequently, some became traders and took control of cotton ginning and sartorial retail.[[36]](#cite_note-39)

From 1900 to 1920, a [sleeping sickness](/wiki/African_trypanosomiasis "African trypanosomiasis") epidemic in the southern part of Uganda, along the north shores of Lake Victoria, killed more than 250,000 people.[[37]](#cite_note-40)

[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") encouraged the colonial administration of Uganda to recruit 77,143 soldiers to serve in the [King's African Rifles](/wiki/King%27s_African_Rifles "King's African Rifles").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] They were seen in action in the [Western Desert campaign](/wiki/Western_Desert_campaign "Western Desert campaign"), the [Abyssinian campaign](/wiki/East_African_campaign_(World_War_II) "East African campaign (World War II)"), the [Battle of Madagascar](/wiki/Battle_of_Madagascar "Battle of Madagascar") and the [Burma campaign](/wiki/Burma_campaign "Burma campaign").

### Independence (1962–1965)

Uganda gained independence from the UK on 9 October 1962 with [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II") as head of state and [Queen of Uganda](/wiki/Queen_of_Uganda "Queen of Uganda"). In October 1963, Uganda became a republic but maintained its membership in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").

The first post-independence election, held in 1962, was won by an alliance between the [Uganda People's Congress](/wiki/Uganda_People%27s_Congress "Uganda People's Congress") (UPC) and [Kabaka Yekka](/wiki/Kabaka_Yekka "Kabaka Yekka") (KY). UPC and KY formed the first post-independence government with [Milton Obote](/wiki/Milton_Obote "Milton Obote") as executive prime minister, with the Buganda Kabaka (King) [Edward Muteesa II](/wiki/Mutesa_II_of_Buganda "Mutesa II of Buganda") holding the largely ceremonial position of president.[[38]](#cite_note-41)[[39]](#cite_note-42)

### Buganda crisis (1962–1966)

Main article: [Mengo Crisis](/wiki/Mengo_Crisis "Mengo Crisis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Owen_Falls_Dam_construction.jpg/250px-Owen_Falls_Dam_construction.jpg)](/wiki/File:Owen_Falls_Dam_construction.jpg)

Uganda's immediate post-independence years were dominated by the relationship between the central government and the largest regional kingdom, [Buganda](/wiki/Buganda "Buganda").[[40]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Kabaka_Muwenda_Mutebi_II_ne_Nnaabagereka_Sylvia_Nagginda.jpg/250px-Kabaka_Muwenda_Mutebi_II_ne_Nnaabagereka_Sylvia_Nagginda.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kabaka_Muwenda_Mutebi_II_ne_Nnaabagereka_Sylvia_Nagginda.jpg)

Kabaka Muwenda Mutebi II and his wife Sylvia Nagginda

The issue of how to manage the largest monarchy within the framework of a unitary state had been a problem since the British creation of the Protectorate of Uganda. Colonial governors had failed to come up with a formula that worked. This was further complicated by Buganda's nonchalant attitude to its relationship with the central government. Buganda never sought independence but rather appeared to be comfortable with a loose arrangement that guaranteed them privileges above the other subjects within the protectorate or a special status when the British left. This was evidenced in part by hostilities between the British colonial authorities and Buganda prior to independence.[[41]](#cite_note-44)

Within Buganda, there were divisions between those who wanted the [Kabaka](/wiki/Kabaka_of_Buganda "Kabaka of Buganda") to remain a dominant monarch and those who wanted to join with the rest of Uganda to create a modern secular state. The split resulted in the creation of two dominant Buganda based parties – the Kabaka Yekka (Kabaka Only) KY, and the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(Uganda) "Democratic Party (Uganda)") (DP) that had roots in the Catholic Church. The bitterness between these two parties was extremely intense especially as the first elections for the post-Colonial parliament approached. The Kabaka particularly disliked the DP leader, [Benedicto Kiwanuka](/wiki/Benedicto_Kiwanuka "Benedicto Kiwanuka").[[42]](#cite_note-45)

Outside Buganda, a soft-spoken politician from Northern Uganda, [Milton Obote](/wiki/Milton_Obote "Milton Obote"), had forged an alliance of non-Buganda politicians to form the Uganda People's Congress (UPC). The UPC at its heart was dominated by politicians who wanted to rectify what they saw as the regional inequality that favoured Buganda's special status. This drew in substantial support from outside Buganda. The party, however, remained a loose alliance of interests, but Obote showed great skill at negotiating them into a common ground based on a federal formula.[[43]](#cite_note-Kasozi-46)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Uganda_Printers_Kampala_1950s.jpg/250px-Uganda_Printers_Kampala_1950s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uganda_Printers_Kampala_1950s.jpg)

The Uganda printers building on Kampala Road, Kampala, Uganda

At Independence, the Buganda question remained unresolved. Uganda was one of the few colonial territories that achieved independence without a dominant political party with a clear majority in parliament. In the pre-Independence elections, the UPC ran no candidates in Buganda and won 37 of the 61 directly elected seats (outside Buganda). The DP won 24 seats outside Buganda. The "special status" granted to Buganda meant that the 21 Buganda seats were elected by proportional representation reflecting the elections to the Buganda parliament – the Lukikko. KY won a resounding victory over DP, winning all 21 seats.

The UPC reached a peak at the end of 1964 when the leader of the DP in parliament, [Basil Kiiza Bataringaya](/wiki/Basil_Kiiza_Bataringaya "Basil Kiiza Bataringaya"), crossed the parliamentary floor with five other MPs, leaving DP with only nine seats. The DP MPs were not particularly happy that the hostility of their leader, Benedicto Kiwanuka, towards the Kabaka was hindering their chances of compromise with KY.[[44]](#cite_note-47) The trickle of defections turned into a flood when 10 KY members crossed the floor when they realised the formal coalition with the UPC was no longer viable. Obote's charismatic speeches across the country were sweeping all before him, and the UPC was winning almost every local election held and increasing its control over all district councils and legislatures outside Buganda.[[45]](#cite_note-48) The response from the Kabaka was mute – probably content in his ceremonial role and symbolism in his part of the country. However, there were also major divisions within his palace that made it difficult for him to act effectively against Obote. By the time Uganda had become independent, Buganda "was a divided house with contending social and political forces"[[46]](#cite_note-Kasozi63-49) There were however problems brewing inside the UPC. As its ranks swelled, the ethnic, religious, regional, and personal interests began to shake the party. The party's apparent strength was eroded in a complex sequence of factional conflicts in its central and regional structures. And by 1966, the UPC was tearing itself apart. The conflicts were further intensified by the newcomers who had crossed the parliamentary floor from DP and KY.[[47]](#cite_note-50)

The UPC delegates arrived in [Gulu](/wiki/Gulu "Gulu") in 1964 for their delegates conference. Here was the first demonstration as to how Obote was losing control of his party. The battle over the Secretary-General of the party was a bitter contest between the new moderate's candidate – [Grace Ibingira](/wiki/Grace_Ibingira "Grace Ibingira") and the radical John Kakonge. Ibingira subsequently became the symbol of the opposition to Obote within the UPC. This is an important factor when looking at the subsequent events that led to the crisis between Buganda and the Central government. For those outside the UPC (including KY supporters), this was a sign that Obote was vulnerable. Keen observers realised the UPC was not a cohesive unit.[[48]](#cite_note-51)

The collapse of the UPC-KY alliance openly revealed the dissatisfaction Obote and others had about Buganda's "special status". In 1964, the government responded to demands from some parts of the vast Buganda Kingdom that they were not the Kabaka's subjects. Prior to colonial rule, Buganda had been rivalled by the neighbouring [Bunyoro](/wiki/Bunyoro "Bunyoro") kingdom. Buganda had conquered parts of Bunyoro and the British colonialists had formalised this in the Buganda Agreements. Known as the "lost counties", the people in these areas wished to revert to being part of Bunyoro. Obote decided to allow a referendum, which angered the Kabaka and most of the rest of Buganda. The residents of the counties voted to return to Bunyoro despite the Kabaka's attempts to influence the vote.[[49]](#cite_note-52) Having lost the referendum, KY opposed the bill to pass the counties to Bunyoro, thus ending the alliance with the UPC.

The UPC, which had previously been a national party, began to break along tribal lines when Ibingira challenged Obote in the UPC. The "North/South" ethnic divide that had been evident in economic and social spheres now entrenched itself in politics. Obote surrounded himself with mainly northern politicians, while Ibingira's supporters who were subsequently arrested and jailed with him, were mainly from the South. In time, the two factions acquired ethnic labels – "Bantu" (the mainly Southern Ibingira faction) and "Nilotic" (the mainly Northern Obote faction). The perception that the government was at war with the Bantu was further enhanced when Obote arrested and imprisoned the mainly Bantu ministers who backed Ibingira.[[50]](#cite_note-Otunnu-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/SA18157-Crowned_Crane_-_%C5%BBuraw_Kr%C3%B3lewski.jpg/250px-SA18157-Crowned_Crane_-_%C5%BBuraw_Kr%C3%B3lewski.jpg)](/wiki/File:SA18157-Crowned_Crane_-_%C5%BBuraw_Kr%C3%B3lewski.jpg)

[Grey Crowned Crane](/wiki/Grey_crowned_crane "Grey crowned crane") – a symbol of Uganda

These labels brought into the mix two very powerful influences. First Buganda – the people of Buganda are Bantu and therefore naturally aligned to the Ibingira faction. The Ibingira faction further advanced this alliance by accusing Obote of wanting to overthrow the Kabaka.[[50]](#cite_note-Otunnu-53) They were now aligned to opposing Obote. Second – the security forces – the British colonialists had recruited the army and police almost exclusively from Northern Uganda due to their perceived suitability for these roles. At independence, the army and police was dominated by northern tribes – mainly Nilotic. They would now feel more affiliated to Obote, and he took full advantage of this to consolidate his power. In April 1966, Obote passed out eight hundred new army recruits at [Moroto](/wiki/Moroto_Town "Moroto Town"), of whom seventy per cent came from the Northern Region.[[51]](#cite_note-54)

At the time, there was a tendency to perceive central government and security forces as dominated by "northerners," particularly the Acholi who through the UPC had significant access to government positions at the national level.[[52]](#cite_note-LRA-MR-55) In northern Uganda there were also varied degrees of anti-Buganda feelings, particularly over the kingdom's "special status" before and after independence, and all the economic and social benefits that came with this status. "Obote brought significant numbers of northerners into the central state, both through the civil service and military, and created a patronage machine in Northern Uganda".[[52]](#cite_note-LRA-MR-55) However, both "Bantu" and "Nilotic" labels represent significant ambiguities. The Bantu category for example includes both Buganda and Bunyoro – historically bitter rivals. The Nilotic label includes the Lugbara, Acholi, and Langi, all of whom have bitter rivalries that were to define Uganda's military politics later. Despite these ambiguities, these events unwittingly brought to fore the northerner/southerner political divide which to some extent still influences Ugandan politics.

The UPC fragmentation continued as opponents sensed Obote's vulnerability. At local level where the UPC dominated most councils discontent began to challenge incumbent council leaders. Even in Obote's home district, attempts were made to oust the head of the local district council in 1966. A more worrying fact for the UPC was that the next national elections loomed in 1967 – and without the support of KY (who were now likely to back the DP), and the growing factionalism in the UPC, there was the real possibility that the UPC would be out of power in months.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Obote went after KY with a new act of parliament in early 1966 that blocked any attempt by KY to expand outside Buganda. KY appeared to respond in parliament through one of their few remaining MPs, the terminally ill Daudi Ochieng. Ochieng was an irony – although from Northern Uganda, he had risen high in the ranks of KY and become a close confidant to the Kabaka who had gifted him with large land titles in Buganda. In Obote's absence from Parliament, Ochieng laid bare the illegal plundering of ivory and gold from the Congo that had been orchestrated by Obote's army chief of staff, Colonel [Idi Amin](/wiki/Idi_Amin "Idi Amin"). He further alleged that Obote, Onama and Neykon had all benefited from the scheme.[[53]](#cite_note-56) Parliament overwhelmingly voted in favour of a motion to censure Amin and investigate Obote's involvement. This shook the government and raised tensions in the country.

KY further demonstrated its ability to challenge Obote from within his party at the UPC Buganda conference, where the Attorney General, Godfrey Binaisa, was ousted by a faction believed to have the backing of KY, Ibingira and other anti-Obote elements in Buganda.[[46]](#cite_note-Kasozi63-49) Obote's response was to arrest Ibingira and other ministers at a cabinet meeting and to assume special powers in February 1966. In March 1966, Obote also announced that the offices of President and vice-president would cease to exist – effectively dismissing the Kabaka. Obote also gave Amin more power – giving him the Army Commander position over the previous holder (Opolot) who had relations to Buganda through marriage (possibly believing Opolot would be reluctant to take military action against the Kabaka if it came to that). Obote abolished the constitution and effectively suspended elections due in a few months. Obote went on television and radio to accuse the Kabaka of various offences including requesting foreign troops which appears to have been explored by the Kabaka following the rumours of Amin plotting a coup. Obote further dismantled the authority of the Kabaka by announcing among other measures:

* The abolition of independent public service commissions for federal units. This removed the Kabaka's authority to appoint civil servants in Buganda.
* The abolition of the Buganda High Court – removing any judicial authority the Kabaka had.
* The bringing of Buganda financial management under further central control.
* Abolition of lands for Buganda chiefs. Land is one of the key sources of Kabaka's power over his subjects.

This set the stage for a conflict between Buganda and the central government. Within Buganda's political institutions, rivalries driven by religion and personal ambition made the institutions ineffective and unable to respond to the central government moves. The Kabaka was often regarded as aloof and unresponsive to advice from the younger Buganda politicians who better understood the new post-Independence politics, unlike the traditionalists who were ambivalent to what was going on as long as their traditional benefits were maintained. The Kabaka favoured the neo-traditionalists.[[54]](#cite_note-57)

In May 1966, the Kabaka asked for foreign help, and the Buganda parliament demanded that the Uganda government leave Buganda, including the capital, Kampala. In response Obote ordered Idi Amin to attack the Kabaka's palace. The battle for the Kabaka's palace was fierce – the Kabaka's guards putting up more resistance than had been expected. The British trained Captain – the Kabaka with about 120 armed men kept Idi Amin at bay for twelve hours.[[55]](#cite_note-58) It is estimated that up to 2,000 people died in the battle which ended when the army called in heavier guns and overran the palace. The anticipated countryside uprising in Buganda did not materialise and a few hours later a beaming Obote met the press to relish his victory. The Kabaka escaped over the palace walls and was transported into exile in London by supporters. He died there three years later.

### Before the coup (1966–1971)

In 1966, following a power struggle between the Obote-led government and King Muteesa, Obote suspended the constitution and removed the ceremonial president and vice-president. In 1967, a new constitution proclaimed Uganda a republic and abolished the traditional kingdoms. Obote was declared the president.[[23]](#cite_note-Background-26)

### Idi Amin's regime (1971–1979)

Main article: [Second Republic of Uganda](/wiki/Second_Republic_of_Uganda "Second Republic of Uganda")

After a [military coup on 25 January 1971](/wiki/1971_Ugandan_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1971 Ugandan coup d'état"), Obote was deposed from power and General [Idi Amin](/wiki/Idi_Amin "Idi Amin") seized control of the country. Amin ruled Uganda as dictator with the support of the military for the next eight years.[[56]](#cite_note-loc-59) He carried out mass killings within the country to maintain his rule. An estimated 80,000–500,000 Ugandans died during his regime.[[57]](#cite_note-60) Aside from his brutalities, he [forcibly removed](/wiki/Expulsion_of_Asians_in_Uganda_in_1972 "Expulsion of Asians in Uganda in 1972") the entrepreneurial [Indian](/wiki/Indian_people "Indian people") minority from Uganda.[[58]](#cite_note-61) In June 1976, Palestinian terrorists hijacked an [Air France](/wiki/Air_France "Air France") flight and forced it to land at [Entebbe airport](/wiki/Entebbe_International_Airport "Entebbe International Airport"). One hundred of the 250 passengers originally on board were held hostage until an [Israeli commando raid](/wiki/Operation_Entebbe "Operation Entebbe") rescued them ten days later.[[59]](#cite_note-62) Amin's reign was ended after the [Uganda–Tanzania War](/wiki/Uganda%E2%80%93Tanzania_War "Uganda–Tanzania War") in 1979, in which Tanzanian forces aided by Ugandan exiles invaded Uganda.

### 1979–present

Main article: [History of Uganda (1979–present)](/wiki/History_of_Uganda_(1979%E2%80%93present) "History of Uganda (1979–present)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Second_Congo_War_Africa_map_en.svg/500px-Second_Congo_War_Africa_map_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Second_Congo_War_Africa_map_en.svg)

Belligerents of the Second Congo War. On 19 December 2005, the [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") found against Uganda, in a case brought by the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo"), for illegal invasion of its territory, and violation of human rights.[[60]](#cite_note-63)

In 1980, the [Ugandan Bush War](/wiki/Ugandan_Bush_War "Ugandan Bush War") broke out, leading to [Yoweri Museveni](/wiki/Yoweri_Museveni "Yoweri Museveni") becoming president, as his forces toppled the previous regime in January 1986.[[61]](#cite_note-64)

[Political parties in Uganda](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Uganda "List of political parties in Uganda") were restricted in their activities beginning that year, in a measure ostensibly designed to reduce sectarian violence. In the [non-party](/wiki/Non-partisan_democracy "Non-partisan democracy") "Movement" system instituted by Museveni, political parties continued to exist, but they could operate only a headquarters office. They could not open branches, hold rallies, or field candidates directly (although electoral candidates could belong to political parties). A constitutional referendum cancelled this nineteen-year ban on multi-party politics in July 2005.

In 1993, [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") visited Uganda during his six-day [pastoral trip](/wiki/List_of_pastoral_visits_of_Pope_John_Paul_II "List of pastoral visits of Pope John Paul II") to urge Ugandans to seek reconciliation.

In the mid-to-late 1990s, Museveni was lauded by Western countries as part of a new generation of African leaders.[[62]](#cite_note-65) His presidency has been marred, however, by invading and occupying the Democratic Republic of the Congo during the [Second Congo War](/wiki/Second_Congo_War "Second Congo War"), resulting in an estimated 5.4 million deaths since 1998, and by participating in other conflicts in the [Great Lakes region of Africa](/wiki/Great_Lakes_(Africa) "Great Lakes (Africa)"). He has struggled for years in the civil war against the Lord's Resistance Army, which has been guilty of numerous crimes against humanity, including [child slavery](/wiki/Child_slavery "Child slavery"), the [Atiak massacre](/wiki/Atiak_massacre "Atiak massacre"), and other mass murders. Conflict in northern Uganda has killed thousands and displaced millions.[[63]](#cite_note-66)

Parliament abolished presidential term limits in 2005, allegedly because Museveni used public funds to pay US$2,000 to each member of parliament who supported the measure.[[64]](#cite_note-67) Presidential [elections](/wiki/2006_Ugandan_general_election "2006 Ugandan general election") were held in February 2006. Museveni ran against several candidates, the most prominent of them being [Kizza Besigye](/wiki/Kizza_Besigye "Kizza Besigye").

On 20 February 2011, the Uganda Electoral Commission declared Museveni the winning candidate of the 2011 [elections](/wiki/2011_Ugandan_general_election "2011 Ugandan general election") that were held on 18 February 2011. The opposition however, were not satisfied with the results, condemning them as full of sham and rigging. According to the official results, Museveni won with 68 per cent of the votes. This easily topped his nearest challenger, Besigye, who had been Museveni's physician and told reporters that he and his supporters "downrightly snub" the outcome as well as the unremitting rule of Museveni or any person he may appoint. Besigye added that the rigged elections would definitely lead to an illegitimate leadership and that it is up to Ugandans to critically analyse this. The European Union's Election Observation Mission reported on improvements and flaws of the Ugandan electoral process: "The electoral campaign and polling day were conducted in a peaceful manner. However, the electoral process was marred by avoidable administrative and logistical failures that led to an unacceptable number of Ugandan citizens being disfranchised."[[65]](#cite_note-68)

Since August 2012, hacktivist group [Anonymous](/wiki/Anonymous_(group) "Anonymous (group)") has threatened Ugandan officials and hacked official government websites over its anti-gay bills.[[66]](#cite_note-69) Some international donors have threatened to cut financial aid to the country if anti-gay bills continue.[[67]](#cite_note-70)

Indicators of a plan for succession by the president's son, Muhoozi Kainerugaba, have increased tensions.[[68]](#cite_note-71)[[69]](#cite_note-72)[[70]](#cite_note-Uganda_2012_Human_Rights_Report-73)[[71]](#cite_note-74)

Museveni has ruled the country since 1986 and he was last re-elected in the January 2021 presidential [elections](/wiki/2021_Ugandan_general_election "2021 Ugandan general election").[[72]](#cite_note-75) According to official results Museveni won the elections with 58% of the vote while popstar-turned-politician [Bobi Wine](/wiki/Bobi_Wine "Bobi Wine") had 35%. The opposition challenged the result because of allegations of widespread fraud and irregularities.[[73]](#cite_note-76)[[74]](#cite_note-77) Another opposition candidate was 24-year-old John Katumba.[[75]](#cite_note-78)

## Geography

Main articles: [Geography of Uganda](/wiki/Geography_of_Uganda "Geography of Uganda") and [Wildlife of Uganda](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Uganda "Wildlife of Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Uganda%2C_2005.jpg/330px-Uganda%2C_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uganda,_2005.jpg)

Relief map of Uganda

Uganda is located in southeast Africa between 1º S and 4º N latitude, and between 30º E and 35º E longitude. Its geography is very diverse, consisting of volcanic hills, mountains, and lakes. The country sits at an average of 900 meters above sea level. Both the eastern and western borders of Uganda have mountains. The Ruwenzori mountain range contains the highest peak in Uganda, which is named Alexandra and measures 5,094 metres (16,713 ft).

### Lakes and rivers

Much of the south of the country is heavily influenced by one of the world's biggest lakes, Lake Victoria, which contains many islands. The most important cities are located in the south, near this lake, including the capital [Kampala](/wiki/Kampala "Kampala")[[76]](#cite_note-79)[[77]](#cite_note-Amendment_2005-80) and the nearby city of [Entebbe](/wiki/Entebbe "Entebbe").[[78]](#cite_note-DataBasin-81) [Lake Kyoga](/wiki/Lake_Kyoga "Lake Kyoga") is in the centre of the country and is surrounded by extensive marshy areas.[[79]](#cite_note-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Evening%2C_Nile_River%2C_Uganda.jpg/250px-Evening%2C_Nile_River%2C_Uganda.jpg)](/wiki/File:Evening,_Nile_River,_Uganda.jpg)

Great Nile River, Uganda

Although landlocked, Uganda contains many large lakes. Besides Lakes Victoria and Kyoga, there are [Lake Albert](/wiki/Lake_Albert_(Africa) "Lake Albert (Africa)"), [Lake Edward](/wiki/Lake_Edward "Lake Edward"), and the smaller [Lake George](/wiki/Lake_George_(Uganda) "Lake George (Uganda)").[[78]](#cite_note-DataBasin-81) It lies almost completely within the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile") basin. The Victoria Nile drains from Lake Victoria into Lake Kyoga and thence into Lake Albert on the Congolese border. It then runs northwards into [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan"). An area in eastern Uganda is drained by the [Suam River](/wiki/Turkwel_River "Turkwel River"), part of the internal drainage basin of [Lake Turkana](/wiki/Lake_Turkana "Lake Turkana"). The extreme north-eastern part of Uganda drains into the Lotikipi Basin, which is primarily in Kenya.[[78]](#cite_note-DataBasin-81)

### Biodiversity and conservation

Main article: [Conservation in Uganda](/wiki/Conservation_in_Uganda "Conservation in Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Koppen-Geiger_Map_UGA_present.svg/330px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_UGA_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_UGA_present.svg)

Uganda map of Köppen climate classification

Uganda has [60 protected areas](/wiki/List_of_Protected_Areas_in_Uganda "List of Protected Areas in Uganda"), including ten national parks: [Bwindi Impenetrable National Park](/wiki/Bwindi_Impenetrable_National_Park "Bwindi Impenetrable National Park") and [Rwenzori Mountains National Park](/wiki/Rwenzori_Mountains_National_Park "Rwenzori Mountains National Park") (both UNESCO [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site")[[80]](#cite_note-83)), [Kibale National Park](/wiki/Kibale_National_Park "Kibale National Park"), [Kidepo Valley National Park](/wiki/Kidepo_Valley_National_Park "Kidepo Valley National Park"), [Lake Mburo National Park](/wiki/Lake_Mburo_National_Park "Lake Mburo National Park"), [Mgahinga Gorilla National Park](/wiki/Mgahinga_Gorilla_National_Park "Mgahinga Gorilla National Park"), [Mount Elgon National Park](/wiki/Mount_Elgon_National_Park "Mount Elgon National Park"), [Murchison Falls National Park](/wiki/Murchison_Falls_National_Park "Murchison Falls National Park"), [Queen Elizabeth National Park](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_National_Park "Queen Elizabeth National Park"), and [Semuliki National Park](/wiki/Semuliki_National_Park "Semuliki National Park").

Uganda is home to a vast number of species, including a population of [mountain gorillas](/wiki/Mountain_gorillas "Mountain gorillas") in the Bwindi Impenetrable National Park, gorillas and [golden monkeys](/wiki/Golden_monkey "Golden monkey") in the Mgahinga Gorilla National Park, and [hippos](/wiki/Hippo "Hippo") in the Murchison Falls National Park.[[81]](#cite_note-84)
[Jackfruit](/wiki/Jackfruit "Jackfruit") can also be found throughout the country.[[82]](#cite_note-85)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/033_Male_Ugandan_kob_trying_to_seduce_a_female_at_Queen_Elizabeth_National_Park_Photo_by_Giles_Laurent.jpg/250px-033_Male_Ugandan_kob_trying_to_seduce_a_female_at_Queen_Elizabeth_National_Park_Photo_by_Giles_Laurent.jpg)](/wiki/File:033_Male_Ugandan_kob_trying_to_seduce_a_female_at_Queen_Elizabeth_National_Park_Photo_by_Giles_Laurent.jpg)

Male Ugandan kob trying to seduce a female at Queen Elizabeth National Park Photo

The country had a 2019 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 4.36/10, ranking it 128th globally out of 172 countries.[[83]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-86)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Politics of Uganda](/wiki/Politics_of_Uganda "Politics of Uganda") and [Elections in Uganda](/wiki/Elections_in_Uganda "Elections in Uganda")

The [President of Uganda](/wiki/President_of_Uganda "President of Uganda") is both [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"). The president appoints a vice-president and a [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Uganda "Prime Minister of Uganda") to aid them in governing.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Yoweri_Museveni_September_2015.jpg/250px-Yoweri_Museveni_September_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yoweri_Museveni_September_2015.jpg)

Yoweri Museveni, president of the republic of Uganda since 1986

The [Parliament of Uganda](/wiki/Parliament_of_Uganda "Parliament of Uganda") has 557 members. These include [constituency representatives](/wiki/List_of_constituencies_in_Uganda "List of constituencies in Uganda"), district woman representatives and representatives of the Uganda People's Defense Forces. There are also 5 representatives of the youth, 5 representatives of workers, 5 representatives of persons with disabilities, and 18 [ex officio](/wiki/Ex_officio_member "Ex officio member") members.[[84]](#cite_note-87)

[Freedom of the press](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Uganda#Freedom_of_the_press "Human rights in Uganda") has been continually infringed upon through the use of intimidation and the Public Order Management Act (2013).[[85]](#cite_note-88) Attacks on political freedom in the country, including the arrest and beating of opposition members of parliament, have led to international criticism, culminating in May 2005 in a decision by the British government to withhold part of its aid to the country. The arrest of the main opposition leader [Kizza Besigye](/wiki/Kizza_Besigye "Kizza Besigye") and the siege of the [High Court](/w/index.php?title=High_Court_(Uganda)&action=edit&redlink=1 "High Court (Uganda) (page does not exist)") during a hearing of Besigye's case by heavily armed security forces – before the [February 2006 elections](/wiki/2006_Ugandan_general_election "2006 Ugandan general election") – led to condemnation.[[86]](#cite_note-hrw1-89)

### Foreign relations

Further information: [Foreign relations of Uganda](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Uganda "Foreign relations of Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/BushMuseveniEntebbe2003.jpg/250px-BushMuseveniEntebbe2003.jpg)](/wiki/File:BushMuseveniEntebbe2003.jpg)

US President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") met with President Yoweri Museveni in Entebbe, Uganda, 11 July 2003.

Uganda is a member of the [East African Community](/wiki/East_African_Community "East African Community") (EAC), along with Kenya, Tanzania, Rwanda, Burundi, and South Sudan. According to the East African Common Market Protocol of 2010, the free trade and free movement of people is guaranteed, including the right to reside in another member country for purposes of employment. This protocol, however, has not been implemented because of work permit and other bureaucratic, legal, and financial obstacles. Uganda is a founding member of the [Intergovernmental Authority on Development](/wiki/Intergovernmental_Authority_on_Development "Intergovernmental Authority on Development") (IGAD), an eight-country bloc including governments from the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa"), [Nile Valley](/wiki/Nile_Valley "Nile Valley"), and the [African Great Lakes](/wiki/African_Great_Lakes "African Great Lakes").[[87]](#cite_note-90) Its headquarters are in Djibouti City. Uganda is also a member of the [Organization of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organization_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organization of Islamic Cooperation").[[88]](#cite_note-oicmems-91)

In August 2025, Uganda made an agreement with the US to take in asylum seekers deported from the US.[[89]](#cite_note-92)
Migrants were to be of African origin and had to have no criminal record.[[90]](#cite_note-ap-93) Critics including [Muwada Nkunyingi](/wiki/Muwada_Nkunyingi "Muwada Nkunyingi") have likened this arrangement to [human trafficking](/wiki/Human_trafficking "Human trafficking").[[90]](#cite_note-ap-93)

### Military

Further information: [Uganda People's Defence Force](/wiki/Uganda_People%27s_Defence_Force "Uganda People's Defence Force")

In Uganda, the [Uganda People's Defence Force](/wiki/Uganda_People%27s_Defence_Force "Uganda People's Defence Force") serves as the military. The [number of military personnel in Uganda](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_military_and_paramilitary_personnel "List of countries by number of military and paramilitary personnel") is estimated at 45,000 soldiers on active duty. The Uganda army is involved in several peacekeeping and combat missions in the region, with commentators noting that only the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") is deployed in more countries. Uganda has soldiers deployed in the northern and eastern areas of the Democratic Republic of the Congo and in the [Central African Republic](/wiki/Central_African_Republic "Central African Republic"), [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"), and [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan").[[91]](#cite_note-94)

### Corruption

Main article: [Corruption in Uganda](/wiki/Corruption_in_Uganda "Corruption in Uganda")

[Transparency International](/wiki/Transparency_International "Transparency International") has rated Uganda's public sector as one of the most corrupt in the world. In 2016, Uganda ranked 151st best out of 176 and had a score of 25 on a scale from 0 (perceived as most corrupt) to 100 (perceived as clean).[[92]](#cite_note-CPI-95)

The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank")'s 2015 Worldwide Governance Indicators ranked Uganda in the worst 12 percentile of all countries.[[93]](#cite_note-96) According to the [United States Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State")'s 2012 Human Rights Report on Uganda, "The World Bank's most recent Worldwide Governance Indicators reflected corruption was a severe problem" and that "the country annually loses 768.9 billion shillings ($286 million) to corruption."[[70]](#cite_note-Uganda_2012_Human_Rights_Report-73)

Ugandan parliamentarians in 2014 earned 60 times what was earned by most state employees, and they sought a major increase. This caused widespread criticism and protests, including the smuggling of two piglets into the parliament in June 2014 to highlight corruption amongst members of parliament. The protesters, who were arrested, used the word "MPigs" to highlight their grievance.[[94]](#cite_note-UganadaMPs-97)

A specific scandal, which had significant international consequences and highlighted the presence of corruption in high-level government offices, was the embezzlement of $12.6 million of donor funds from the Office of the Prime Minister in 2012. These funds were "earmarked as crucial support for rebuilding northern Uganda, ravaged by a 20-year war, and Karamoja, Uganda's poorest region." This scandal prompted the EU, the United Kingdom, Germany, Denmark, Ireland, and Norway to suspend aid.[[95]](#cite_note-98)

Widespread grand and petty corruption involving public officials and political patronage systems have also seriously affected the investment climate in Uganda. One of the high corruption risk areas is the public procurement in which non-transparent under-the-table cash payments are often demanded from procurement officers.[[96]](#cite_note-99)

What may ultimately compound this problem is the availability of oil. The Petroleum Bill, passed by parliament in 2012 and touted by the NRM as bringing transparency to the oil sector, has failed to please domestic and international political commentators and economists. For instance, Angelo Izama, a Ugandan energy analyst at the US-based Open Society Foundation said the new law was tantamount to "handing over an ATM (cash) machine" to Museveni and his regime.[[97]](#cite_note-100) According to Global Witness in 2012, a non-governmental organisation devoted to international law, Uganda now has "oil reserves that have the potential to double the government's revenue within six to ten years, worth an estimated US $2.4 billion per year."[[98]](#cite_note-101)

The Non-Governmental Organisations (Amendment) Act, passed in 2006, has stifled the productivity of NGOs through erecting barriers to entry, activity, funding and assembly within the sector. Burdensome and corrupt registration procedures (i.e. requiring recommendations from government officials; annual re-registration), unreasonable regulation of operations (i.e. requiring government notification prior to making contact with individuals in NGO's area of interest), and the precondition that all foreign funds be passed through the Bank of Uganda, among other things, are severely limiting the output of the NGO sector.

A leaked confidential document exposes scandals in Uganda's Office of the Prime Minister, including distribution of rotten food, excessive spending, land grabbing allegations, and controversial handling of political matters. These revelations raise questions about the office's ethics and transparency and involves Charles Odongtho, the OPM's public relations expert.[[99]](#cite_note-102)

### Human rights

Main article: [Human rights in Uganda](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Uganda "Human rights in Uganda")

There are many areas which continue to attract concern when it comes to human rights in Uganda.

Conflict in the northern parts of the country continues to generate reports of abuses by both the rebel [Lord's Resistance Army](/wiki/Lord%27s_Resistance_Army "Lord's Resistance Army") (LRA), led by [Joseph Kony](/wiki/Joseph_Kony "Joseph Kony"), and the [Ugandan Army](/wiki/Uganda_People%27s_Defence_Force "Uganda People's Defence Force"). A UN official accused the LRA in February 2009 of "appalling brutality" in the Democratic Republic of Congo.[[100]](#cite_note-103)

The number of [internally displaced persons](/wiki/Internally_displaced_persons "Internally displaced persons") is estimated at 1.4 million. Torture continues to be a widespread practice amongst security organisations.
[Child labour](/wiki/Child_labour "Child labour") is common in Uganda. Many child workers are active in agriculture.[[101]](#cite_note-unhcr.org-104) Children who work on tobacco farms in Uganda are exposed to health hazards.[[101]](#cite_note-unhcr.org-104) Child domestic servants in Uganda risk [sexual abuse](/wiki/Sexual_abuse "Sexual abuse").[[101]](#cite_note-unhcr.org-104) [Trafficking of children](/wiki/Trafficking_of_children "Trafficking of children") occurs.[[101]](#cite_note-unhcr.org-104) [Slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") and [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour "Forced labour") are prohibited by the Ugandan constitution.[[101]](#cite_note-unhcr.org-104)

The US Committee for Refugees and Immigrants reported several violations of refugee rights in 2007, including forcible deportations by the Ugandan government and violence directed against refugees.[[102]](#cite_note-World_Refugee_Survey_2008-105)

Torture and extrajudicial killings have been a pervasive problem in Uganda in recent years. For instance, according to a 2012 US State Department report, "the African Center for Treatment and Rehabilitation for Torture Victims registered 170 allegations of torture against police, 214 against the UPDF, 1 against military police, 23 against the Special Investigations Unit, 361 against unspecified security personnel, and 24 against prison officials" between January and September 2012.[[70]](#cite_note-Uganda_2012_Human_Rights_Report-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Hillary_Rodham_Clinton_with_2011_Human_Rights_Defender_Award_recipients.jpg/250px-Hillary_Rodham_Clinton_with_2011_Human_Rights_Defender_Award_recipients.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hillary_Rodham_Clinton_with_2011_Human_Rights_Defender_Award_recipients.jpg)

Hillary Rodham Clinton with 2011 Human Rights Defender Award recipients

In September 2009, Museveni refused Kabaka Muwenda Mutebi, the Baganda king, permission to visit some areas of Buganda Kingdom, particularly the Kayunga district. Riots occurred and over 40 people were killed while others still remain imprisoned. Furthermore, 9 more people were killed during the April 2011 "Walk to Work" demonstrations. According to the Humans Rights Watch 2013 World Report on Uganda, the government has failed to investigate the killings associated with both of these events.[[103]](#cite_note-106)

#### LGBT rights

Main article: [LGBT rights in Uganda](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Uganda "LGBT rights in Uganda")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(September 2023)* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Uganda_Anti-Homosexuality_Bill_protest.jpg/250px-Uganda_Anti-Homosexuality_Bill_protest.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uganda_Anti-Homosexuality_Bill_protest.jpg)

A 2009 protest in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") against Uganda's [first Anti-Homosexuality Bill](/wiki/Anti-Homosexuality_Act,_2014 "Anti-Homosexuality Act, 2014")

In 2007, a newspaper, the *[Red Pepper](/wiki/Red_Pepper_(newspaper) "Red Pepper (newspaper)")*, published a list of allegedly gay men; as a result, many of the men listed suffered harassment.[[104]](#cite_note-107)

On 9 October 2010, the Ugandan newspaper *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone_(Uganda) "Rolling Stone (Uganda)")* published a front-page article titled "100 Pictures of Uganda's Top Homos Leak" that listed the names, addresses, and photographs of 100 homosexuals alongside a yellow banner that read "Hang Them."[[105]](#cite_note-Guardian-108) The paper also alleged [homosexual recruitment](/wiki/Homosexual_recruitment "Homosexual recruitment") of Ugandan children. The publication attracted international attention and criticism from human rights organisations, such as [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International"),[[106]](#cite_note-109) [No Peace Without Justice](/wiki/No_Peace_Without_Justice "No Peace Without Justice")[[107]](#cite_note-110) and the [International Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Trans and Intersex Association](/wiki/International_Lesbian,_Gay,_Bisexual,_Trans_and_Intersex_Association "International Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Trans and Intersex Association").[[108]](#cite_note-ILGA-111) According to [gay rights](/wiki/Gay_rights "Gay rights") activists, many Ugandans have been attacked since the publication.[[109]](#cite_note-Independent-112) On 27 January 2011, gay rights activist [David Kato](/wiki/David_Kato "David Kato") was murdered.[[110]](#cite_note-BBC-113)

In 2009, the Ugandan parliament considered an [Anti-Homosexuality Bill](/wiki/Uganda_Anti-Homosexuality_Bill "Uganda Anti-Homosexuality Bill") which would have broadened the criminalisation of homosexuality by introducing the death penalty for people who have previous convictions, or are HIV-positive, and engage in same-sex sexual acts. The bill included provisions for Ugandans who engage in same-sex sexual relations outside of Uganda, asserting that they may be [extradited](/wiki/Extradition "Extradition") back to Uganda for punishment, and included penalties for individuals, companies, media organisations, or non-governmental organisations that support legal protection for homosexuality or sodomy. On 14 October 2009, MP [David Bahati](/wiki/David_Bahati "David Bahati") submitted the [private member's bill](/wiki/Private_member%27s_bill "Private member's bill") and was believed to have had widespread support in the Uganda parliament.[[111]](#cite_note-Harpers2010-114) The hacktivist group [Anonymous](/wiki/Anonymous_(hacker_group) "Anonymous (hacker group)") hacked into Ugandan government websites in protest of the bill.[[112]](#cite_note-115) In response to global condemnation the debate of the bill was delayed, but it was eventually passed on 20 December 2013 and President Museveni signed it on 24 February 2014. The death penalty was dropped in the final legislation. The law was widely condemned by the international community. Denmark, the Netherlands, and Sweden said they would withhold aid. On 28 February 2014, the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") said it would postpone a US$90 million loan, while the United States said it was reviewing ties with Uganda.[[113]](#cite_note-WorldBankPostponesLOan-116) On 1 August 2014, the [Constitutional Court of Uganda](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Uganda "Constitutional Court of Uganda") ruled the bill invalid as it was not passed with the required [quorum](/wiki/Quorum "Quorum").[[114]](#cite_note-117)[[115]](#cite_note-118)[[116]](#cite_note-119) A 13 August 2014 news report said that the Ugandan attorney general had dropped all plans to appeal, per a directive from President Museveni who was concerned about foreign reaction to the bill and who also said that any newly introduced bill should not criminalise same-sex relationships between consenting adults.[[117]](#cite_note-120) As of 2019, adoption of LGBT rights on the African continent was slow but progressing with South Africa being the only country where same sex marriages are recognised.[[118]](#cite_note-121)

##### Anti-Homosexuality Act, 2023

Main article: [Anti-Homosexuality Act, 2023](/wiki/Anti-Homosexuality_Act,_2023 "Anti-Homosexuality Act, 2023")

On 9 March 2023, [Asuman Basalirwa](/wiki/Asuman_Basalirwa "Asuman Basalirwa") (a [member of parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Uganda "Parliament of Uganda") since 2018 from the opposition representing [Bugiri](/wiki/Bugiri "Bugiri") Municipality on Justice Forum party ticket) tabled a proposed law which sought to castigate gay sex and "the promotion or recognition of such relations" and he made remarks that, "In this country, or in this world, we talk about human rights. But it is also true that there are human wrongs. I want to submit that homosexuality is a human wrong that offends the laws of Uganda and threatens the sanctity of the family, the safety of our children and the continuation of humanity through reproduction."[[119]](#cite_note-122) The speaker of parliament, [Annet Anita Among](/wiki/Anita_Among "Anita Among"), referred the bill to a house committee for scrutiny, the first step in an accelerated process to pass the proposal into law. The parliament speaker had earlier noted that: "We want to appreciate our promoters of homosexuality for the social economic development they have brought to the country," in reference to western countries and donors. "But we do not appreciate the fact that they are killing morals. We do not need their money, we need our culture." - during a prayer service held in parliament and attended by several religious leaders.[[120]](#cite_note-123) The Speaker vowed to pass the bill into law at whatever cost to shield Uganda's culture and its sovereignty.[[121]](#cite_note-124)

On 21 March 2023, the Ugandan parliament approved a bill that would have made identifying as homosexual punishable by 20 year in prison and "aggravated homosexuality" by the death penalty.[[122]](#cite_note-125)[[123]](#cite_note-126)[[124]](#cite_note-lancet-127) The bill was not approved by the President and, in the revised version which became law, identifying as homosexual was not a crime.

The [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") strongly condemned the bill. During a [White House Press](/wiki/White_House_Press_Secretary "White House Press Secretary") briefing on 22 March 2023, [Karine Jean-Pierre](/wiki/Karine_Jean-Pierre "Karine Jean-Pierre") stated. "Human rights [are universal](/wiki/Moral_universalism "Moral universalism"). No one should be attacked, imprisoned, or killed simply because of who they are or whom they love."[[125]](#cite_note-128) Further criticism came from the United Kingdom,[[126]](#cite_note-129) Canada,[[127]](#cite_note-130) Germany,[[128]](#cite_note-131) and the European Union.

### Refugee policy

Main article: [Refugees in Uganda](/wiki/Refugees_in_Uganda "Refugees in Uganda")

Uganda’s approach to hosting refugees is among the most progressive globally.[[129]](#cite_note-briefi-132) Despite limited international support, the country maintains an open door policy that is anchored in national law. Refugees have access to essential services, may apply for work permits and move freely within the country. They are allotted a plot of land and may live in settlements that are closely integrated with surrounding Ugandan communities. They are also allowed to form associations and civil society groups.[[130]](#cite_note-onthef-133)[[131]](#cite_note-welhav-134) In this setting, many organisations created and run by refugees have developed, providing a range of support to their communities. These groups nonetheless face challenges such as scarce funding, barriers to registration and limited influence over decisions.[[130]](#cite_note-onthef-133) The refugee policy does not provide a path to Ugandan citizenship.[[131]](#cite_note-welhav-134)

Uganda has a long history of receiving [displaced people](/wiki/Forced_displacement "Forced displacement"). The country hosted refugees even before its independence in 1962, including groups fleeing [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and later people escaping violence in [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda") and southern [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan"). Uganda also experienced periods when large numbers of its own citizens were displaced, particularly during the 1970s and 1980s and during the insurgency by the [Lord’s Resistance Army](/wiki/Lord%E2%80%99s_Resistance_Army "Lord’s Resistance Army").[[130]](#cite_note-onthef-133) Regional conflicts throughout the 1990s and 2000s, including the [Rwandan genocide](/wiki/Rwandan_genocide "Rwandan genocide"), [Sudanese civil war](/wiki/The_Second_Sudanese_Civil_War "The Second Sudanese Civil War") and the [Congolese wars](/wiki/Congolese_Civil_War "Congolese Civil War"), continued to drive people into Uganda. By 2014, the country sheltered roughly 400,000 displaced people.[[130]](#cite_note-onthef-133)

By October 2025, Uganda hosted roughly 1.95 million refugees,[[132]](#cite_note-ugasud-135) more than any other country in Africa.[[130]](#cite_note-onthef-133) Most are rural groups mainly from [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan") and the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo"), as well as from [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"), [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi"), and [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda").[[129]](#cite_note-briefi-132)[[130]](#cite_note-onthef-133) From 2023, more than 90,000 refugees fleeing the [civil war in Sudan](/wiki/Sudanese_civil_war_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Sudanese civil war (2023–present)") have entered.[[132]](#cite_note-ugasud-135)

### Administrative divisions

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Uganda](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Uganda "Administrative divisions of Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Ug-map.PNG/250px-Ug-map.PNG)](/wiki/File:Ug-map.PNG)

Some of the districts in Uganda

As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Uganda&action=edit), Uganda is divided into four [Regions of Uganda](/wiki/Regions_of_Uganda "Regions of Uganda") and 136 [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Uganda "Districts of Uganda").[[133]](#cite_note-district-136)[[134]](#cite_note-factsheet-137) Rural areas of districts are subdivided into [sub-counties](/wiki/Sub-counties_of_Uganda "Sub-counties of Uganda"), parishes, and villages. Municipal and town councils are designated in urban areas of districts.[[135]](#cite_note-138)

Political subdivisions in Uganda are officially served and united by the [Uganda Local Governments Association](/wiki/Uganda_Local_Governments_Association "Uganda Local Governments Association") (ULGA), a voluntary and non-profit body which also serves as a forum for support and guidance for Ugandan sub-national governments.[[136]](#cite_note-139)

Parallel with the state administration, five traditional [Bantu](/wiki/Bantu_peoples "Bantu peoples") kingdoms have remained, enjoying some degrees of mainly cultural autonomy. The kingdoms are [Toro](/wiki/Toro_Kingdom "Toro Kingdom"), [Busoga](/wiki/Busoga "Busoga"), [Bunyoro](/wiki/Bunyoro "Bunyoro"), [Buganda](/wiki/Buganda "Buganda"), and [Rwenzururu](/wiki/Rwenzururu "Rwenzururu"). Furthermore, some groups attempt to restore [Ankole](/wiki/Ankole "Ankole") as one of the officially recognised traditional kingdoms, to no avail yet.[[137]](#cite_note-140) Several other kingdoms and chiefdoms are officially recognised by the government, including the union of Alur chiefdoms, the Iteso paramount chieftaincy, the paramount chieftaincy of Lango and the Padhola state.[[138]](#cite_note-141)

## Economy and infrastructure

Main articles: [Economy of Uganda](/wiki/Economy_of_Uganda "Economy of Uganda"), [Energy in Uganda](/wiki/Energy_in_Uganda "Energy in Uganda"), and [Agriculture in Uganda](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Uganda "Agriculture in Uganda")

The [Bank of Uganda](/wiki/Bank_of_Uganda "Bank of Uganda") is the [central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") of Uganda and handles monetary policy along with the printing of the [Ugandan shilling](/wiki/Ugandan_shilling "Ugandan shilling").[[139]](#cite_note-142)

In 2015, Uganda's economy generated export income from the following merchandise: coffee (US$402.63 million), oil re-exports (US$131.25 million), base metals and products (US$120.00 million), fish (US$117.56 million), maize (US$90.97 million), cement (US$80.13 million), tobacco (US$73.13 million), tea (US$69.94 million), sugar (US$66.43 million), hides and skins (US$62.71 million), cocoa beans (US$55.67 million), beans (US$53.88 million), [simsim](/wiki/Sesame "Sesame") (US$52.20 million), flowers (US$51.44 million), and other products (US$766.77 million).[[140]](#cite_note-143)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/GDP_per_capita_development_of_Uganda.svg/250px-GDP_per_capita_development_of_Uganda.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_of_Uganda.svg)

Change in per capita GDP of Uganda, 1950–2018. Figures are inflation-adjusted to 2011 International dollars.

The country has been experiencing consistent economic growth. In fiscal year 2015–16, Uganda recorded gross domestic product growth of 4.6 per cent in real terms and 11.6 per cent in nominal terms. This compares to 5.0 per cent real growth in fiscal year 2014–15.[[141]](#cite_note-144): vii

The country has largely untapped reserves of both [crude oil](/wiki/Crude_oil "Crude oil") and natural gas.[[142]](#cite_note-oilrush-145) While agriculture accounted for 56 per cent of the economy in 1986, with coffee as its main export, it has now been surpassed by the services sector, which accounted for 52 per cent of GDP in 2007.[[143]](#cite_note-146) In the 1950s, the British colonial regime encouraged some 500,000 subsistence farmers to join co-operatives.[[144]](#cite_note-147) Since 1986, the government (with the support of foreign countries and international agencies) has acted to rehabilitate an economy devastated during the regime of Idi Amin[[145]](#cite_note-148) and the subsequent civil war.[[146]](#cite_note-cia-149)

In 2012, the World Bank listed Uganda on the [Heavily Indebted Poor Countries](/wiki/Heavily_Indebted_Poor_Countries "Heavily Indebted Poor Countries") list.[[147]](#cite_note-150)

Economic growth has not always led to [poverty reduction](/wiki/Poverty_reduction "Poverty reduction"). Despite an average annual growth of 2.5 per cent between 2000 and 2003, poverty levels increased by 3.8 per cent during that time.[[148]](#cite_note-ODI-151) This has highlighted the importance of avoiding [jobless growth](/wiki/Jobless_growth "Jobless growth") and is part of the rising awareness in development circles of the need for [equitable growth](/wiki/Development_economics#equitable_growth "Development economics") not just in Uganda, but across the developing world.[[148]](#cite_note-ODI-151)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Uganda_GV5_lo_%284108162183%29.jpg/250px-Uganda_GV5_lo_%284108162183%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uganda_GV5_lo_(4108162183).jpg)

Coffee fields in southwestern Uganda

With the Uganda securities exchanges established in 1996, several equities have been listed. The government has used the stock market as an avenue for privatisation. All government treasury issues are listed on the securities exchange. The Capital Markets Authority has licensed 18 brokers, asset managers, and investment advisors including: African Alliance Investment Bank, Baroda Capital Markets Uganda Limited, Crane Financial Services Uganda Limited, Crested Stocks and Securities Limited, Dyer & Blair Investment Bank, Equity Stock Brokers Uganda Limited, Renaissance Capital Investment Bank and UAP Financial Services Limited.[[149]](#cite_note-152) As one of the ways of increasing formal domestic savings, pension sector reform is the centre of attention (2007).[[150]](#cite_note-153)[[151]](#cite_note-154)

Uganda traditionally depends on Kenya for access to the Indian Ocean port of [Mombasa](/wiki/Mombasa "Mombasa"). Efforts have intensified to establish a second access route to the sea via the lakeside ports of [Bukasa](/wiki/Bukasa "Bukasa") in Uganda and [Musoma](/wiki/Musoma "Musoma") in Tanzania, connected by railway to [Arusha](/wiki/Arusha "Arusha") in the Tanzanian interior and to the port of [Tanga](/wiki/Tanga,_Tanzania "Tanga, Tanzania") on the Indian Ocean.[[152]](#cite_note-155)
Uganda is a member of the [East African Community](/wiki/East_African_Community "East African Community") and a potential member of the planned [East African Federation](/wiki/East_African_Federation "East African Federation").

Uganda has a large diaspora, residing mainly in the United States and the United Kingdom. This diaspora has contributed enormously to Uganda's economic growth through remittances and other investments (especially property). According to the World Bank, Uganda received in 2016 an estimated US$1.099 billion in remittances from abroad, second only to Kenya (US$1.574 billion) in the East African Community,[[153]](#cite_note-156) and seventh in Africa.[[154]](#cite_note-157) Uganda also serves as an economic hub for a number of neighbouring countries like the Democratic Republic of the Congo,[[155]](#cite_note-158) South Sudan,[[156]](#cite_note-159) and Rwanda.[[157]](#cite_note-160)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Uganda_Strassennetz.svg/250px-Uganda_Strassennetz.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Uganda_Strassennetz.svg)

Roads in Uganda

The Ugandan Bureau of Statistics announced inflation was 4.6 per cent in November 2016.[[158]](#cite_note-161) On 29 June 2018, Uganda's statistics agency said the country registered a drop in inflation to 3.4 per cent in the financial year ending 2017/18 compared to the 5.7 per cent recorded in the financial year 2016/17.[[159]](#cite_note-162)

### Industry

Uganda ranked as [number 102](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") among the countries of the world in nominal [Gross Domestic Product](/wiki/Gross_Domestic_Product "Gross Domestic Product") by the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") with a [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") of 26,349 (US$million).[[160]](#cite_note-GDP_by_IMF-163) The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") ranked Uganda as [number 99](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") in nominal [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") with a [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") of 25,891 (US$million).[[161]](#cite_note-GDP_by_World_Bank-164)
Based on the GDP with [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") the [IMF](/wiki/IMF "IMF") ranked Uganda as [number 86](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)") (91,212 million of current Int$) and the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") [ranked them 90](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)") (79,889 million of current Int$).[[160]](#cite_note-GDP_by_IMF-163)[[161]](#cite_note-GDP_by_World_Bank-164)

Since the 1990s, the economy in Uganda has grown. Real gross domestic product (GDP) grew at an average of 6.7% annually during the period 1990–2015, whereas real GDP per capita grew at 3.3% per annum during the same period.[[162]](#cite_note-165)

### Poverty

Main article: [Poverty in Uganda](/wiki/Poverty_in_Uganda "Poverty in Uganda")
