[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Kif20180421_114559.jpg/250px-Kif20180421_114559.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kif20180421_114559.jpg)

One of the schools located at the Nile River

Uganda is one of the poorest nations in the world. In 2012, 37.8 per cent of the population lived on less than $1.25 a day.[[163]](#cite_note-166) Despite making enormous progress in reducing the countrywide poverty incidence from 56 per cent of the population in 1992 to 24.5 per cent in 2009, poverty remains deep-rooted in the country's rural areas, which are home to 84 per cent of Ugandans.[[164]](#cite_note-167)

People in rural areas of Uganda depend on farming as the main source of income and 90 per cent of all rural women work in the agricultural sector.[[165]](#cite_note-168) In addition to agricultural work, rural women are responsible for the caretaking of their families. The average Ugandan woman spends 9 hours a day on domestic tasks, such as preparing food and clothing, fetching water and firewood, and caring for the elderly, the sick as well as orphans. Women on average work longer hours than men, between 12 and 18 hours per day, with a mean of 15 hours, as compared to men, who work between 8 and 10 hours a day, although urban men and women work very similar hours. In 2005, 26% of households were headed by women only (FHH), up from previous years as a result of death of men from AIDS. There were most FHHs in the top quintile, by income, (31%). Male only headed households in poverty were also increased to a similar level as FHH, though little research had been done.[[166]](#cite_note-169)

To supplement their income, rural women may engage in small-scale entrepreneurial activities such as rearing and selling local breeds of animals. Nonetheless, because of their heavy workload, they have little time for these income-generating activities. The poor cannot support their children at school and in most cases, girls drop out of school to help out in domestic work or to get married. Other girls engage in sex work. As a result, young women tend to have older and more sexually experienced partners and this puts women at a disproportionate risk of getting affected by HIV, accounting for about 5.7 per cent of all adults living with HIV in Uganda.[[167]](#cite_note-170)

Maternal health in rural Uganda lags behind national policy targets and the [Millennium Development Goals](/wiki/Millennium_Development_Goals "Millennium Development Goals"), with geographical inaccessibility, lack of transport and financial burdens identified as key demand-side constraints to accessing maternal health services;[[168]](#cite_note-171) as such, interventions like intermediate transport mechanisms have been adopted as a means to improve women's access to maternal health care services in rural regions of the country.[[169]](#cite_note-172)

Gender inequality is the main hindrance to reducing women's poverty. Women are subjected to an overall lower social status than men. Many women believe this reduces their power to act independently, participate in community life, become educated and escape reliance upon abusive men.[[170]](#cite_note-173)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Entebbe_Airport.JPG/250px-Entebbe_Airport.JPG)](/wiki/File:Entebbe_Airport.JPG)

Entebbe International Airport

### Air transportation

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(July 2024)* |

There are [36 airports](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Uganda "List of airports in Uganda") in Uganda. Commercial airlines operate scheduled passenger services out of four airports. Uganda currently has one functioning international airport, [Entebbe International Airport](/wiki/Entebbe_International_Airport "Entebbe International Airport"), which is located 25 miles (40 km) south-west of [Kampala](/wiki/Kampala "Kampala"). In 2017, the airport traffic was 1.53 million passengers, 8% more than the previous year.[[171]](#cite_note-174) A second international airport, [Hoima International Airport](/wiki/Hoima_International_Airport "Hoima International Airport"), is currently under construction.[[172]](#cite_note-175)

### Road network

See also: [List of roads in Uganda](/wiki/List_of_roads_in_Uganda "List of roads in Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Wild_route.jpg/250px-Wild_route.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wild_route.jpg)

Road to Murchison

Roads are the main method of transportation in Uganda. 95% of freight and passenger traffic is handled by road traffic. The road network in Uganda is approximately 129,469 km (80,448 mi) long. About 4% of these roads are paved which equates to only about 5,300 kilometres (3,300 mi) of paved road. The different types of roads are national roads (22,009 km (13,676 mi)—17%), district roads (33,661 km (20,916 mi)—26%), urban roads (9,062 km (5,631 mi)—7%), and community roads (64,734 km (40,224 mi)—50%).[[173]](#cite_note-176) The national roads make up about 17% of the road network but carry over 80% of the total road traffic.[[174]](#cite_note-177) In Uganda there are 83,000 private cars which means 2.94 cars per 1000 inhabitants.[[175]](#cite_note-178)

### Railroad

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Uganda_railways_assessment_2010-2.jpg/250px-Uganda_railways_assessment_2010-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uganda_railways_assessment_2010-2.jpg)

Uganda Railway

The rail network in Uganda is approximately 1,260 kilometres (780 mi) long. The longest lines are the main line from [Kampala](/wiki/Kampala "Kampala") to [Tororo](/wiki/Tororo "Tororo") (249 kilometres (155 mi)), the western line from [Kampala](/wiki/Kampala "Kampala") to [Kasese](/wiki/Kasese "Kasese") (333 kilometres (207 mi)), the northern line from [Tororo](/wiki/Tororo "Tororo") to [Pakwach](/wiki/Pakwach "Pakwach") (641 kilometres (398 mi)).[[176]](#cite_note-179)

### Communications

Main article: [Communications in Uganda](/wiki/Communications_in_Uganda "Communications in Uganda")

There are several systems of communication, including telephony, radio and television broadcasts, internet, mail, and newspapers. The use of phones and the internet has rapidly increased.

There are seven telecommunications companies and, as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Uganda&action=edit), over 24 million subscribers[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] according to the [Uganda Communications Commission](/wiki/Uganda_Communications_Commission "Uganda Communications Commission"),[[177]](#cite_note-3R-180)[[178]](#cite_note-:0-181) in a population of 48 million.[[179]](#cite_note-:1-182) More than 95% of internet connections are made using mobile phones.[[180]](#cite_note-183)

### Energy

See also: [Energy in Uganda](/wiki/Energy_in_Uganda "Energy in Uganda")

Uganda has abundant energy resources, which are fairly distributed throughout the country. These include hydropower, biomass, solar, geothermal, peat and fossil fuels.

In the 1980s, the majority of energy in Uganda came from charcoal and wood. However, oil was found in the [Lake Albert](/wiki/Lake_Albert_(Africa) "Lake Albert (Africa)") area, totalling an estimated 95 million cubic metres (3.4×10~~^~~9 cu ft) of crude.[[142]](#cite_note-oilrush-145) [Heritage Oil](/wiki/Heritage_Oil "Heritage Oil") discovered one of the largest crude oil finds in Uganda, and continues operations there.[[181]](#cite_note-184)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Uganda_PVOUT_Photovoltaic-power-potential-map_GlobalSolarAtlas_World-Bank-Esmap-Solargis.png/250px-Uganda_PVOUT_Photovoltaic-power-potential-map_GlobalSolarAtlas_World-Bank-Esmap-Solargis.png)](/wiki/File:Uganda_PVOUT_Photovoltaic-power-potential-map_GlobalSolarAtlas_World-Bank-Esmap-Solargis.png)

Uganda PVOUT photovoltaic power potential map

Uganda and Tanzania signed a deal on 13 September 2016 for the two countries build a 1,445 km, $3.5bn crude oil pipeline. The [Uganda–Tanzania Crude Oil Pipeline](/wiki/East_African_Crude_Oil_Pipeline "East African Crude Oil Pipeline") (UTCOP), also known as the East African Crude Oil Pipeline (EACOP), will be the first of its kind in East Africa, will connect Uganda's oil-rich Hoima region with the Indian Ocean through the Tanga port in Tanzania.

Uganda's favourable enabling environment and broad presence of private sector investment presents a unique opportunity to deliver on [Power Africa](/wiki/Power_Africa "Power Africa") goals.[[182]](#cite_note-185) Uganda is one of the few sub-Saharan African countries to have liberalised and financially viable energy markets, with generation, transmission and supply segments unbundled since 2001.[[183]](#cite_note-186) There is an independent [Electricity Regulatory Authority](/wiki/Electricity_Regulatory_Authority "Electricity Regulatory Authority") that undertakes sector regulation and oversight. The largest distribution company, [UMEME](/wiki/Umeme "Umeme") is privately owned and has a 20-year concession for distribution and retail. The country, however, is divided into 13 rural service territories, and 6 of these are being managed by small distribution companies. [Independent power producers](/wiki/Independent_power_producer "Independent power producer") (IPPs) currently account for nearly 60% of generation capacity. Issues with integrated planning and the financial ecosystem persist.

### Water supply and sanitation

Main article: [Water supply and sanitation in Uganda](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Uganda "Water supply and sanitation in Uganda")

According to a 2006 published report, the Ugandan water supply and sanitation sector had made substantial progress in urban areas since the mid-1990s, with substantial increases in coverage as well as in operational and commercial performance.[[184]](#cite_note-Mugisha-187): 3–4 Sector reforms in the period 1998–2003 included the commercialisation and modernisation of the [National Water and Sewerage Corporation](/wiki/National_Water_and_Sewerage_Corporation "National Water and Sewerage Corporation") operating in cities and larger towns, as well as decentralisation and private sector participation in small towns.[[185]](#cite_note-188): 15

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Rivers_and_lakes_of_Uganda.png/250px-Rivers_and_lakes_of_Uganda.png)](/wiki/File:Rivers_and_lakes_of_Uganda.png)

Main Rivers and Lakes of Uganda

Although these reforms have attracted significant international attention, 38 per cent of the population still had no access to an [improved water source](/wiki/Improved_water_source "Improved water source") in 2010. Concerning access to [improved sanitation](/wiki/Improved_sanitation "Improved sanitation"), figures have varied widely. According to government figures, it was 70 per cent in rural areas and 81 per cent in urban areas in 2011;[[186]](#cite_note-SPR_2011-189) according to UN figures, it was only 34 per cent.[[187]](#cite_note-JMP-190)

The water and sanitation sector was recognised as a key area under the 2004 Poverty Eradication Action Plan (PEAP), Uganda's
main strategy paper to fight poverty.[[188]](#cite_note-PEAP-191): 182–188 According to a 2006 published report, a comprehensive expenditure framework had been introduced to co-ordinate financial support by external donors, the national government, and [nongovernmental organisations](/wiki/NGO "NGO").[[189]](#cite_note-MWE_SWAP-192): 5 The PEAP estimated that from 2001 to 2015, about US$1.4 billion, or US$92 million per year, was needed to increase water supply coverage up to 95 per cent, with rural areas needing US$956 million, urban areas and large towns needing US$281 million, and small towns needing US$136 million.[[188]](#cite_note-PEAP-191): 182–183

### Education

Main article: [Education in Uganda](/wiki/Education_in_Uganda "Education in Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Makerere_University_Campus_Entrance_Road.JPG/250px-Makerere_University_Campus_Entrance_Road.JPG)](/wiki/File:Makerere_University_Campus_Entrance_Road.JPG)

Makerere university Campus Entrance

Uganda's educational system, while lacking in many areas, has seen significant change since the late 1990s. The educational system is set up so that children spend seven years in primary school, six years in secondary school, and three to five years in post secondary school. In 1997, the government declared that primary school would be free for all children.[[190]](#cite_note-193) This amendment has had huge benefits. In 1986, only two million children were attending primary school. By 1999, six million children were attending primary school, and this number has continued to climb.

Following significant gains in access to primary education since 1997 when universal primary education (UPE) was introduced, Uganda in 2007 became the first country in sub-Saharan Africa to introduce universal secondary education (USE).[[191]](#cite_note-194) This led to an increase in lower secondary enrolment of nearly 25% between 2007 and 2012.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Uganda_students.jpg/250px-Uganda_students.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uganda_students.jpg)

Students in Uganda

At the 2002 census, Uganda had a [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") rate of 66.8 per cent (76.8 per cent male and 57.7 per cent female).[[146]](#cite_note-cia-149) Public spending on education was at 5.2 per cent of the 2002–2005 GDP.[[192]](#cite_note-hdrstats.undp.org-195)

As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Uganda&action=edit), the [NCHE](/wiki/Uganda_National_Council_for_Higher_Education "Uganda National Council for Higher Education") website listed 46 private accredited universities,[[193]](#cite_note-196) including [Makerere University](/wiki/Makerere_University "Makerere University"), [Mbarara University of science and technology](/wiki/Mbarara_University "Mbarara University"), [Kyambogo University](/wiki/Kyambogo_University "Kyambogo University"), [Gulu University](/wiki/Gulu_University "Gulu University"), [Uganda Christian University](/wiki/Uganda_Christian_University "Uganda Christian University"), [Kampala international University](/wiki/Kampala_International_University "Kampala International University").

### Health

Main articles: [Health in Uganda](/wiki/Health_in_Uganda "Health in Uganda") and [HIV/AIDS in Uganda](/wiki/HIV/AIDS_in_Uganda "HIV/AIDS in Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Aerial_view_of_old_Mulago_Hospital_in_Uganda.jpg/250px-Aerial_view_of_old_Mulago_Hospital_in_Uganda.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_view_of_old_Mulago_Hospital_in_Uganda.jpg)

Mulago National Referral Hospital

There were eight physicians per 100,000 persons in the early 2000s.[[192]](#cite_note-hdrstats.undp.org-195) Uganda's elimination of user fees at state health facilities in 2001 has resulted in an 80 per cent increase in visits, with over half of this increase coming from the poorest 20 per cent of the population.[[194]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-197) This policy has been cited as a key factor in helping Uganda achieve its Millennium Development Goals and as an example of the importance of equity in achieving those goals.[[148]](#cite_note-ODI-151) Despite this policy, many users are denied care if they do not provide their own medical equipment, as happened in the highly publicised case of Jennifer Anguko.[[195]](#cite_note-198) Poor communication within hospitals,[[196]](#cite_note-199) low satisfaction with health services[[197]](#cite_note-200) and distance to health service providers undermine the provision of quality health care to people living in Uganda, and particularly for those in poor and elderly-headed households.[[198]](#cite_note-pariyo2009-201) The provision of subsidies for poor and rural populations, along with the extension of public private partnerships, have been identified as important provisions to enable vulnerable populations to access health services.[[198]](#cite_note-pariyo2009-201)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Life_expectancy_in_Uganda.svg/250px-Life_expectancy_in_Uganda.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Life_expectancy_in_Uganda.svg)

Development of life expectancy

[Life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") at birth was estimated to be 63.4 years in 2019.[[199]](#cite_note-202) The [infant mortality](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality") rate was approximately 61 deaths per 1,000 children in 2012.[[200]](#cite_note-203)

In July 2012, there was an [Ebola](/wiki/Ebola "Ebola") outbreak in the [Kibaale District](/wiki/Kibaale_District "Kibaale District") of the country.[[201]](#cite_note-204) On 4 October 2012, the Ministry of Health officially declared the end of the outbreak after at least 16 people had died.[[202]](#cite_note-205)

The Health Ministry announced on 16 August 2013 that three people had died in northern Uganda from a suspected outbreak of [Congo Crimean Hemorrhagic Fever](/wiki/Congo_Crimean_Hemorrhagic_Fever "Congo Crimean Hemorrhagic Fever").[[203]](#cite_note-206)

Uganda has been among the rare [HIV](/wiki/HIV "HIV") success stories.[[192]](#cite_note-hdrstats.undp.org-195) Infection rates of 30 per cent of the population in the 1980s fell to 6.4 per cent by the end of 2008.[[204]](#cite_note-207)[[205]](#cite_note-208) Meanwhile, the practice of abstinence was found to have decreased.[[206]](#cite_note-209)

Less than half of all sexually active unmarried women use a modern contraceptive method, a fraction that has barely changed from 2000 to 2011. However, only ≈26% of married women used contraceptives in 2011. The use of contraceptives also differs substantially between poor (≈15%) and wealthy women (≈40%).[[207]](#cite_note-:2-210) As a result, Ugandan women have ≈6 children while they prefer to have around ≈4. According to the 2011 Uganda Demographic and Health Survey (DHS), more than 40% of births are unplanned. In 2010, the Ugandan Ministry of Health estimated that unsafe abortion accounted for 8% of the country's maternal deaths.[[207]](#cite_note-:2-210) The 2006 Uganda Demographic Health Survey (UDHS) indicated that roughly 6,000 women die each year from pregnancy-related complications.[[208]](#cite_note-211) Pilot studies in 2012 by Future Health Systems have shown that this rate could be significantly reduced by implementing a voucher scheme for health services and transport to clinics.[[209]](#cite_note-212)[[210]](#cite_note-213)

The prevalence of [female genital mutilation](/wiki/Female_genital_mutilation "Female genital mutilation") (FGM) is low: according to a 2013 UNICEF report,[[211]](#cite_note-UNICEF2013p27-214) only 1 per cent of women in Uganda have undergone FGM, with the practice being illegal in the country.[[212]](#cite_note-215)

### Crime and law enforcement

Main article: [Uganda Police Force](/wiki/Uganda_Police_Force "Uganda Police Force")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Women_of_the_Uganda_Police_Force_marching_during_the_International_Women%27s_Day_celebrations_in_Mbale_Municipality.jpg/250px-Women_of_the_Uganda_Police_Force_marching_during_the_International_Women%27s_Day_celebrations_in_Mbale_Municipality.jpg)](/wiki/File:Women_of_the_Uganda_Police_Force_marching_during_the_International_Women%27s_Day_celebrations_in_Mbale_Municipality.jpg)

Women of Uganda Police Force

The Uganda Police Force is the national [police](/wiki/Police "Police") force. Its head is called the [Inspector General of Police](/wiki/Inspector_General_of_Police "Inspector General of Police") (IGP), currently Abbas Byakagaba. Byakagaba replaced former IGP, [Geoffrey Tumusiime](/wiki/Geoffrey_Tumusiime "Geoffrey Tumusiime") on 18 May 2024.[[213]](#cite_note-18mayIGP-216) Recruitment to the forces is done annually.[[214]](#cite_note-217)

The [Allied Democratic Forces](/wiki/Allied_Democratic_Forces "Allied Democratic Forces") is a violent rebel force that opposes the Ugandan government. These rebels are an enemy of the Uganda People's Defence Force and an affiliate of [Al-Shabaab](/wiki/Al-Shabaab_(militant_group) "Al-Shabaab (militant group)").[[215]](#cite_note-218)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Uganda](/wiki/Tourism_in_Uganda "Tourism in Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Rwenzori_Mountains.jpg/250px-Rwenzori_Mountains.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rwenzori_Mountains.jpg)

Rwenzori mountains in Uganda

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/FLII_Uganda.png/250px-FLII_Uganda.png)](/wiki/File:FLII_Uganda.png)

Forest Landscape Integrity Index 2019 map of Uganda. Forest condition measured by degree of anthropogenic modification. 0 = most modifications; 10 = least. Created in [Google Earth Engine](/wiki/Google_Earth_Engine "Google Earth Engine"). National boundaries = LSIB 2017: Large Scale International Boundary Polygons, Detailed, US Officer of the Geographer.

[Tourism in Uganda](/wiki/Tourism_in_Uganda "Tourism in Uganda") is focused on Uganda's landscape and wildlife. It is a major driver of employment, investment and foreign exchange, contributing 4.9 trillion [Ugandan shillings](/wiki/Ugandan_shilling "Ugandan shilling") (US$1.88 billion or €1.4 billion as of August 2013) to Uganda's [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") in the financial year 2012–13.[[216]](#cite_note-Mutagamba-219) The Uganda Tourism Board is responsible for maintaining information pertaining to tourism in Uganda. The main attractions are photo safaris through the [National parks and game Reserves](/wiki/List_of_protected_areas_of_Uganda "List of protected areas of Uganda"). Other attractions include the Mountain Gorillas found in Bwindi Impenetrable National Park (BINP) and Mgahinga Gorilla National Park (MGNP), Uganda having some of the oldest cultural kingdom in Africa has many [Cultural sites](/wiki/National_Cultural_Sites_of_Uganda "National Cultural Sites of Uganda"). Uganda is a birding paradise boasting a massive bird list of more than 1073 recorded bird species ranking 4th in Africa's bird species and 16th internationally.[[217]](#cite_note-220) Uganda has landscapes ranging from white-capped [Rwenzori mountains](/wiki/Rwenzori_Mountains "Rwenzori Mountains") to the [Great Rift Valley](/wiki/Great_Rift_Valley "Great Rift Valley").

## Science and technology

Main article: [Science and technology in Uganda](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Uganda "Science and technology in Uganda")

The *National Science, Technology and Innovation Policy* dates from 2009. Its overarching goal is to 'strengthen national capability to generate, transfer and apply scientific knowledge, skills and technologies that ensure sustainable utilisation of natural resources for the realisation of Uganda's development objectives.' The policy precedes Uganda Vision 2040, which was launched in April 2013 to transform 'Ugandan society from a peasant to a modern and prosperous country within 30 years,' in the words of the Cabinet. Uganda Vision 2040 vows to strengthen the private sector, improve education and training, modernise infrastructure and the underdeveloped services and agriculture sectors, foster industrialisation and promote good governance, among other goals. Potential areas for economic development include oil and gas, tourism, minerals and information and communication technologies (ICTs).[[218]](#cite_note-:12-221)

Uganda was ranked 124th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025,[[219]](#cite_note-222)[[220]](#cite_note-223) down from 102nd in 2019.[[221]](#cite_note-224) Research funding climbed between 2008 and 2010 from 0.33% to 0.48% of GDP. Over the same period, the number of researchers doubled (in head counts) from 1 387 to 2 823, according to the [UNESCO Institute for Statistics](/wiki/UNESCO_Institute_for_Statistics "UNESCO Institute for Statistics"). This represents a leap from 44 to 83 researchers per million inhabitants over the same period. One in four researchers is a woman.[[218]](#cite_note-:12-221)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Uganda](/wiki/Demographics_of_Uganda "Demographics of Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Population_density_of_Uganda_districts.png/250px-Population_density_of_Uganda_districts.png)](/wiki/File:Population_density_of_Uganda_districts.png)

Population density of Uganda districts

Uganda's population grew from 9.5 million people in 1969 to 34.9 million in 2014, and as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Uganda&action=edit) was measured at 45.9 million.[[9]](#cite_note-census24-10) With respect to the census in 2002, the population increased by 21.7 million people in those 22 years.[[9]](#cite_note-census24-10) Uganda's [median age](/wiki/Median_age "Median age") of 15 years is the lowest in the world.[[146]](#cite_note-cia-149) Uganda has the fifth highest [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") in the world, at 5.97 children born per woman (2014 estimates).[[146]](#cite_note-cia-149)

Of Uganda's population, 23.6 million (51.4 per cent) were female and 22.3 million were male. The census results also showed that 49.5 per cent of Ugandans were under the age of 18, underscoring the country’s very young age structure.[[222]](#cite_note-225)

There were about 80,000 [Indians in Uganda](/wiki/Indians_in_Uganda "Indians in Uganda") before Idi Amin required the [expulsion of Ugandan-Asians](/wiki/Expulsion_of_Asians_from_Uganda "Expulsion of Asians from Uganda") (mostly of Indian origin) in 1972, which reduced the population to as low as 7,000. Many Indians, however, returned to Uganda after Amin's ouster in 1979.[[223]](#cite_note-226) Around 90 per cent of Ugandan Indians reside in Kampala. There is also a population of about 10,000 [white Africans](/wiki/White_Africans "White Africans") and 3,000 Arabs in the country.[[224]](#cite_note-227)

According to the [UNHCR](/wiki/United_Nations_High_Commissioner_for_Refugees "United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees"), Uganda hosts over 1.4 million refugees on its soil as of August 2021.[[225]](#cite_note-:3-228)[[226]](#cite_note-UNHCR_stats-229) Most come from neighbouring countries in the [African Great Lakes](/wiki/African_Great_Lakes "African Great Lakes") region, particularly [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan") (68.0 per cent) and [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/The_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "The Democratic Republic of the Congo") (24.6%).[[226]](#cite_note-UNHCR_stats-229) In August 2021, Uganda received some refugees from Afghanistan following the Taliban takeover.[[225]](#cite_note-:3-228)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Uganda](/wiki/Languages_of_Uganda "Languages of Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Languages_of_Uganda.png/330px-Languages_of_Uganda.png)](/wiki/File:Languages_of_Uganda.png)

An ethnolinguistic map of Uganda

[Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language"), a widely used language throughout the African Great Lakes region, was approved as the country's second official national language in 2005.[[77]](#cite_note-Amendment_2005-80)[[227]](#cite_note-Kagonye_22_sw-230) [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") was the only official language until the constitution was amended in 2005.[[228]](#cite_note-231)[[146]](#cite_note-cia-149)[[77]](#cite_note-Amendment_2005-80)[[1]](#cite_note-Republic-1)[[229]](#cite_note-232) Uganda has its own dialect of English, known as [Uglish](/wiki/Ugandan_English "Ugandan English").[[230]](#cite_note-233)[[231]](#cite_note-234) Although Swahili has not been favoured by the Bantu-speaking populations of the south and south-west of the country, it is an important *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* in the northern regions. It is also widely used in the police and military forces, which may be a historical result of the disproportionate recruitment of northerners into the security forces during the colonial period. The status of Swahili has thus alternated with the political group in power.[[232]](#cite_note-UCLA-235) For example, Idi Amin, who came from the north-west, declared Swahili to be the national language.[[233]](#cite_note-236)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Uganda](/wiki/Religion_in_Uganda "Religion in Uganda")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Rubaga_Cthedral.jpg/250px-Rubaga_Cthedral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rubaga_Cthedral.jpg)

[Saint Mary's Cathedral Rubaga](/wiki/Rubaga_Cathedral "Rubaga Cathedral") is the parent cathedral of the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Kampala](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Kampala "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Kampala").

The [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Uganda "Catholic Church in Uganda") had the largest number of adherents (39.3 per cent, down from 41.6 in 2002), followed by the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") [Church of Uganda](/wiki/Church_of_Uganda "Church of Uganda") (32 per cent, down from 35.9 per cent). The category of [Evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical")/[Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal")/[Born-Again](/wiki/Born-Again "Born-Again") showed the most growth, rising from 4.7% in 2002 to 11.1% in 2018. [Adventist](/wiki/Adventism "Adventism") and other [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") churches claimed most of the remaining Christians, although there was also a small [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") community.[[234]](#cite_note-Census_2014-237)[[235]](#cite_note-Census_2002-238) The next most reported religion of Uganda was [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Uganda "Islam in Uganda"), with Muslims representing 14% per cent of the population, up from 12.1% in 2002.[[236]](#cite_note-239)[[234]](#cite_note-Census_2014-237)

The remainder of the population according to the 2014 census followed traditional religions (0.1 per cent, down from 1% in 2002), other religions (1.4 per cent), or had no religious affiliation (0.2 per cent).[[234]](#cite_note-Census_2014-237)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Cultural_celebrations_resumed_with_the_end_of_the_LRA_conflict_in_Northern_Uganda_%287269658432%29.jpg/250px-Cultural_celebrations_resumed_with_the_end_of_the_LRA_conflict_in_Northern_Uganda_%287269658432%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cultural_celebrations_resumed_with_the_end_of_the_LRA_conflict_in_Northern_Uganda_(7269658432).jpg)

Cultural celebrations in Northern Uganda

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Uganda_-_Ruwenzori_Mountain_Lady.jpg/250px-Uganda_-_Ruwenzori_Mountain_Lady.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uganda_-_Ruwenzori_Mountain_Lady.jpg)

Owing to the large number of communities, culture within Uganda is diverse. Many Asians (mostly from India) who were expelled during the regime of Idi Amin have returned to Uganda.[[237]](#cite_note-240)

### Media

Further information: [Mass media in Uganda](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Uganda "Mass media in Uganda")

Uganda has a number of media outlets that broadcast domestically and globally. They cover news, magazines, sports, business and entertainment.

Popular [Ugandan newspapers](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_Uganda "List of newspapers in Uganda") include:

* *[New Vision](/wiki/New_Vision_(newspaper) "New Vision (newspaper)")*
* *[Daily Monitor](/wiki/Daily_Monitor "Daily Monitor")*
* *[Bukedde](/wiki/Bukedde "Bukedde")*
* *[The Observer](/wiki/The_Observer_(Uganda) "The Observer (Uganda)")*
* *[East African Business Week](/wiki/East_African_Business_Week "East African Business Week")*
* *[Red Pepper](/wiki/Red_Pepper_(newspaper) "Red Pepper (newspaper)")*

The most popular [television stations in Uganda](/wiki/List_of_television_stations_in_Uganda "List of television stations in Uganda") include:

* [Uganda Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Uganda_Broadcasting_Corporation "Uganda Broadcasting Corporation") (UBC)
* [NTV](/wiki/NTV_Uganda "NTV Uganda")
* [NBS Television](/wiki/NBS_Television_(Uganda) "NBS Television (Uganda)")
* [Sanyuka TV](/wiki/Sanyuka_TV_Uganda "Sanyuka TV Uganda")
* [Baba TV](/wiki/Baba_TV "Baba TV")
* Top TV
* Spark TV

All of the media is controlled and regulated under the [Uganda Communications Commission](/wiki/Uganda_Communications_Commission "Uganda Communications Commission") (UCC).[[238]](#cite_note-241)[[239]](#cite_note-242)

### Sports

Further information: [Uganda at the Olympics](/wiki/Uganda_at_the_Olympics "Uganda at the Olympics") and [Uganda at the Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Uganda_at_the_Commonwealth_Games "Uganda at the Commonwealth Games")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Aerobics.jpg/250px-Aerobics.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerobics.jpg)

Aerobics

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is the national sport in Uganda. The [Uganda national football team](/wiki/Uganda_national_football_team "Uganda national football team"), nicknamed "The Cranes", is controlled by the [Federation of Uganda Football Associations](/wiki/Federation_of_Uganda_Football_Associations "Federation of Uganda Football Associations"). They have never qualified for the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") finals. Their best finish in the [African Cup of Nations](/wiki/African_Cup_of_Nations "African Cup of Nations") was second in [1978](/wiki/1978_African_Cup_of_Nations "1978 African Cup of Nations"). Among clubs, [SC Villa](/wiki/SC_Villa "SC Villa") are the most successful, having won the [national league](/wiki/Uganda_Premier_League "Uganda Premier League") 16 times and having reached the final of the [African Cup of Champions Clubs](/wiki/CAF_Champions_League "CAF Champions League") in [1991](/wiki/1991_African_Cup_of_Champions_Clubs "1991 African Cup of Champions Clubs"), a feat also achieved by [Simba SC](/wiki/Simba_SC "Simba SC") in [1972](/wiki/1972_African_Cup_of_Champions_Clubs "1972 African Cup of Champions Clubs"). [KCCA](/wiki/Kampala_Capital_City_Authority_FC "Kampala Capital City Authority FC") are second in national league wins with 13.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Uganda&action=edit), [Uganda at the Olympics](/wiki/Uganda_at_the_Olympics "Uganda at the Olympics") has won a total of two gold, three silver, and two bronze medals, four of which were in [boxing](/wiki/Boxing_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Boxing at the Summer Olympics") and three in [athletics](/wiki/Sport_of_athletics "Sport of athletics"). [At the Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Uganda_at_the_Commonwealth_Games "Uganda at the Commonwealth Games"), Uganda has collected 13 gold medals and a total 49 medals, all in boxing and athletics.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Picture_._Jpg_besketboll.jpg/250px-Picture_._Jpg_besketboll.jpg)](/wiki/File:Picture_._Jpg_besketboll.jpg)

Basketball

The Uganda national boxing team is called The Bombers.[[240]](#cite_note-243) They have won four medals at the Summer Olympics from 1968 to 1980, as well as two medals the [1974 World Amateur Boxing Championships](/wiki/1974_World_Amateur_Boxing_Championships "1974 World Amateur Boxing Championships"). Notable boxers include [Cornelius Boza-Edwards](/wiki/Cornelius_Boza-Edwards "Cornelius Boza-Edwards"), [Justin Juuko](/wiki/Justin_Juuko "Justin Juuko"), [Ayub Kalule](/wiki/Ayub_Kalule "Ayub Kalule"), [John Mugabi](/wiki/John_Mugabi "John Mugabi"), [Eridadi Mukwanga](/wiki/Eridadi_Mukwanga "Eridadi Mukwanga"), [Joseph Nsubuga](/w/index.php?title=Joseph_Nsubuga&action=edit&redlink=1 "Joseph Nsubuga (page does not exist)"), [Kassim Ouma](/wiki/Kassim_Ouma "Kassim Ouma"), [Sam Rukundo](/wiki/Sam_Rukundo "Sam Rukundo") and [Leo Rwabwogo](/wiki/Leo_Rwabwogo "Leo Rwabwogo").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Football_Match_in_Uganda.jpg/250px-Football_Match_in_Uganda.jpg)](/wiki/File:Football_Match_in_Uganda.jpg)

Football match in Uganda

In athletics, [John Akii-Bua](/wiki/John_Akii-Bua "John Akii-Bua") won the first Olympic gold medal for Uganda. At the [1972 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics") in Munich, he won the [400m hurdles](/wiki/400_metres_hurdles "400 metres hurdles") race with a world record time of 47.82 seconds.[[241]](#cite_note-244) 400 metres runner [Davis Kamoga](/wiki/Davis_Kamoga "Davis Kamoga") earned the bronze medal at [1996 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics") in Atlanta and the silver medal at the [1997 World Championships](/wiki/1997_World_Championships_in_Athletics "1997 World Championships in Athletics"). [Dorcus Inzikuru](/wiki/Dorcus_Inzikuru "Dorcus Inzikuru") won the 3000 m steeplechase at the [2005 World Championships](/wiki/2005_World_Championships_in_Athletics "2005 World Championships in Athletics") and the [2006 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2006_Commonwealth_Games "2006 Commonwealth Games").

[Stephen Kiprotich](/wiki/Stephen_Kiprotich "Stephen Kiprotich") has won the marathon at the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") in London and the [2013 World Championships](/wiki/2013_World_Championships_in_Athletics "2013 World Championships in Athletics"), and finished second at the 2015 [Tokyo Marathon](/wiki/Tokyo_Marathon "Tokyo Marathon").[[242]](#cite_note-245) [Joshua Cheptegei](/wiki/Joshua_Cheptegei "Joshua Cheptegei") has won 10 km races at the World Championships, [World Athletics Cross Country Championships](/wiki/World_Athletics_Cross_Country_Championships "World Athletics Cross Country Championships") and [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), and has set world records in 5 km and 15 km. [Halimah Nakaayi](/wiki/Halimah_Nakaayi "Halimah Nakaayi") won the 800 meters race at the 2019 World Championships.

In [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), Uganda was part of the [East Africa team](/wiki/East_African_cricket_team "East African cricket team") that qualified for the [Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup") in [1975](/wiki/1975_Cricket_World_Cup "1975 Cricket World Cup"). Recently [Uganda national cricket team](/wiki/Uganda_national_cricket_team "Uganda national cricket team") qualified for [2024 ICC T20 World Cup](/wiki/2024_ICC_Men%27s_T20_World_Cup "2024 ICC Men's T20 World Cup").

The country has an increasingly successful [national basketball team](/wiki/Uganda_national_basketball_team "Uganda national basketball team"). It is nicknamed "The Silverbacks",[[243]](#cite_note-246) and made its debut at the [2015 FIBA Africa Championship](/wiki/2015_FIBA_Africa_Championship "2015 FIBA Africa Championship").

In July 2011, Kampala, Uganda qualified for the [2011 Little League World Series](/wiki/2011_Little_League_World_Series "2011 Little League World Series") in [Williamsport](/wiki/Williamsport,_Pennsylvania "Williamsport, Pennsylvania"), Pennsylvania for the first time, beating Saudi Arabian baseball team Dharan LL, although visa complications prevented them from attending the series.[[244]](#cite_note-247) Little League teams from Uganda qualified for and attended the [2012 Little League World Series](/wiki/2012_Little_League_World_Series "2012 Little League World Series").[[245]](#cite_note-248)

### Cinema

Main article: [Cinema of Uganda](/wiki/Cinema_of_Uganda "Cinema of Uganda")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(January 2020)* |

The Ugandan film industry is relatively young. It is developing quickly, but still faces an assortment of challenges. There has been support for the industry as seen in the proliferation of film festivals such as [Amakula](/wiki/Amakula_International_Film_Festival "Amakula International Film Festival"), [Pearl International Film Festival](/wiki/Pearl_International_Film_Festival "Pearl International Film Festival"), [Maisha](/wiki/Maisha_Film_Lab "Maisha Film Lab") African Film Festival and Manya Human Rights Festival. However, filmmakers struggle against the competing markets from other countries on the continent such as those in Nigeria and South Africa in addition to the big budget films from Hollywood.[[246]](#cite_note-cannesvupar.com-249)

The first publicly recognised film that was produced solely by Ugandans was *Feelings Struggle*, which was directed and written by Hajji [Ashraf Ssemwogerere](/wiki/Ashraf_Ssemwogerere "Ashraf Ssemwogerere") in 2005.[[247]](#cite_note-250) This marks the year of ascent of film in Uganda, a time when many enthusiasts were proud to classify themselves as cinematographers in varied capacities.[[248]](#cite_note-monitor.co.ug-251)

The local film industry is polarised between two types of filmmakers. The first are filmmakers who use the [Nollywood](/wiki/Nollywood "Nollywood") [video film era](/wiki/Video_film_era "Video film era")'s guerrilla approach to film making, churning out a picture in around two weeks and screening it in makeshift video halls. The second is the filmmaker who has the film aesthetic, but with limited funds has to depend on the competitive scramble for donor cash.[[246]](#cite_note-cannesvupar.com-249)

Though cinema in Uganda is evolving, it still faces major challenges. Along with technical problems such as refining acting and editing skills, there are issues regarding funding and lack of government support and investment. There are no schools in the country dedicated to film, banks do not extend credit to film ventures, and distribution and marketing of movies remains poor.[[246]](#cite_note-cannesvupar.com-249)[[248]](#cite_note-monitor.co.ug-251)

The Uganda Communications Commission (UCC) prepared regulations starting in 2014 that required Ugandan television to broadcast 70 per cent Ugandan content and of this, 40 per cent to be independent productions. With the emphasis on Ugandan film and the UCC regulations favouring Ugandan productions for mainstream television, Ugandan film may become more prominent and successful in the near future.[[248]](#cite_note-monitor.co.ug-251)
